User studies with participants of varying skill levels are performed with ex-vivo tissue to provide clinically realistic data, and a network for surgical skill assessment is employed to demonstrate utilization of the collected data. Through the user study experiments, we obtained a dataset of 214 validated instances across multiple canonical training tasks. All software and data are available at surgsync.github.io.

</details>


### [384] [CN-CBF: Composite Neural Control Barrier Function for Safe Robot Navigation in Dynamic Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.06921)
*Bojan Derajić,Sebastian Bernhard,Wolfgang Hönig*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Neural composite CBF method improves safe robot navigation in dynamic environments with up to 18% higher success rates than baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Safety filtering using control barrier functions (CBFs) is easy to deploy but difficult to design, especially in dynamic environments. Existing learning- and model-based methods have shortcomings that need addressing.

Method: Proposes neural CBF design using composite approach where multiple neural CBFs are combined into one. Individual CBFs trained via Hamilton-Jacobi reachability framework to approximate optimal safe sets for single moving obstacles. Uses residual neural architecture to ensure estimated safe set doesn't intersect with failure set.

Result: Extensive simulation experiments for ground robot and quadrotor show improved success rates up to 18% compared to best baseline methods, without increasing motion conservativeness. Hardware experiments successfully demonstrate method for both robot types.

Conclusion: The proposed neural composite CBF method is simple yet effective for safe robot navigation in dynamic environments, outperforming existing approaches while maintaining practical deployability.

Abstract: Safe navigation of autonomous robots remains one of the core challenges in the field, especially in dynamic and uncertain environments. One of the prevalent approaches is safety filtering based on control barrier functions (CBFs), which are easy to deploy but difficult to design. Motivated by the shortcomings of existing learning- and model-based methods, we propose a simple yet effective neural CBF design method for safe robot navigation in dynamic environments. We employ the idea of a composite CBF, where multiple neural CBFs are combined into a single CBF. The individual CBFs are trained via the Hamilton-Jacobi reachability framework to approximate the optimal safe set for single moving obstacles. Additionally, we use the residual neural architecture, which guarantees that the estimated safe set does not intersect with the corresponding failure set. The method is extensively evaluated in simulation experiments for a ground robot and a quadrotor, comparing it against several baseline methods. The results show improved success rates of up to 18\% compared to the best baseline, without increasing the conservativeness of the motion. Also, the method is demonstrated in hardware experiments for both types of robots.

</details>


### [385] [LIPP: Load-Aware Informative Path Planning with Physical Sampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.06924)
*Hojune Kim,Guangyao Shi,Gaurav S. Sukhatme*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: LIPP extends classical IPP to account for physical sample collection where collected samples add mass and increase energy costs, optimizing routing, visitation order, and sampling counts under energy constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical IPP assumes making measurements doesn't change robot state, but physical sample collection adds mass that increases energy costs for subsequent motion. Existing IPP formulations ignore this coupling, leading to energy-suboptimal plans that collect fewer samples than energy budgets permit.

Method: Formulate Load-aware Informative Path Planning (LIPP) as a Mixed-Integer Quadratic Program (MIQP) that jointly optimizes routing, visitation order, and per-location sampling count under an energy budget, explicitly modeling the coupling between information gain and load-dependent traversal costs.

Result: LIPP strictly generalizes C-IPP, reduces to C-IPP as sample mass approaches zero, provides theoretical bounds on path-length increase relative to C-IPP, and through simulations shows progressive improvement in uncertainty reduction per unit energy as sample mass increases.

Conclusion: LIPP provides a more realistic and energy-efficient approach to informative path planning for physical sample collection missions by explicitly accounting for the mass-dependent energy costs of collected samples.

Abstract: In classical Informative Path Planning (C-IPP), robots are typically modeled as mobile sensors that acquire digital measurements such as images or radiation levels. In this model - since making a measurement leaves the robot's physical state unchanged - traversal costs are determined solely by the path taken. This is a natural assumption for many missions, but does not extend to settings involving physical sample collection, where each collected sample adds mass and increases the energy cost of all subsequent motion. As a result, IPP formulations that ignore this coupling between information gain and load-dependent traversal cost can produce plans that are distance-efficient but energy-suboptimal, collecting fewer samples and less data than the energy budget would permit. In this paper, we introduce Load-aware Informative Path Planning (LIPP ), a generalization of C-IPP that explicitly models this coupling and the resulting order-dependent traversal costs. We formulate LIPP as a Mixed-Integer Quadratic Program (MIQP) that jointly optimizes routing, visitation order, and per-location sampling count under an energy budget. We show that LIPP strictly generalizes C-IPP: as sample unit mass $λ\to 0$, the load-dependent energy model reduces exactly to the classical distance budget constraint, recovering C-IPP as a special case. We further derive theoretical bounds on the path-length increase of LIPP relative to C-IPP, characterizing the trade-off for improved energy efficiency. Finally, through extensive simulations across 2000 diverse mission scenarios, we demonstrate that LIPP matches the behavior of C-IPP at zero sample mass and progressively achieves higher uncertainty reduction per unit energy as sample mass increases.

</details>


### [386] [A Contrastive Fewshot RGBD Traversability Segmentation Framework for Indoor Robotic Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.06927)
*Qiyuan An,Tuan Dang,Fillia Makedon*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Multi-modal few-shot segmentation framework combining RGB images and sparse 1D laser depth to improve indoor traversability segmentation, especially for thin obstacles like chair legs, using negative contrastive learning and attention-based depth alignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pure vision-based models often fail to detect thin obstacles (e.g., chair legs) in indoor environments, posing safety risks for autonomous agents. There's a need for robust traversability segmentation that can generalize from limited labeled data.

Method: Proposes a multi-modal few-shot segmentation framework with: 1) Negative contrastive learning branch using negative prototypes (obstacles) to refine free-space predictions and prevent overfitting, 2) Two-stage attention depth module to align 1D depth vectors with RGB images both horizontally and vertically, 3) Integration of RGB images with sparse 1D laser depth information.

Result: Outperforms state-of-the-art FSS and RGB-D segmentation baselines on custom indoor RGB-D traversability dataset, achieving up to 9% higher mIoU under both 1-shot and 5-shot settings.

Conclusion: The method effectively leverages negative prototypes and sparse depth information for robust and efficient traversability segmentation, demonstrating improved detection of challenging obstacles while requiring minimal labeled data.

Abstract: Indoor traversability segmentation aims to identify safe, navigable free space for autonomous agents, which is critical for robotic navigation. Pure vision-based models often fail to detect thin obstacles, such as chair legs, which can pose serious safety risks. We propose a multi-modal segmentation framework that leverages RGB images and sparse 1D laser depth information to capture geometric interactions and improve the detection of challenging obstacles. To reduce the reliance on large labeled datasets, we adopt the few-shot segmentation (FSS) paradigm, enabling the model to generalize from limited annotated examples. Traditional FSS methods focus solely on positive prototypes, often leading to overfitting to the support set and poor generalization. To address this, we introduce a negative contrastive learning (NCL) branch that leverages negative prototypes (obstacles) to refine free-space predictions. Additionally, we design a two-stage attention depth module to align 1D depth vectors with RGB images both horizontally and vertically. Extensive experiments on our custom-collected indoor RGB-D traversability dataset demonstrate that our method outperforms state-of-the-art FSS and RGB-D segmentation baselines, achieving up to 9\% higher mIoU under both 1-shot and 5-shot settings. These results highlight the effectiveness of leveraging negative prototypes and sparse depth for robust and efficient traversability segmentation.

</details>


### [387] [Failure Mechanisms and Risk Estimation for Legged Robot Locomotion on Granular Slopes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.06928)
*Xingjue Liao,Feifei Qian*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Hexapedal robot study reveals granular slope locomotion failures stem from delayed anchoring and backward slip, not sinkage; physics model predicts failure regimes for safer robot operation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Legged robots struggle on granular slopes like sand dunes due to reduced shear strength and gravity-induced anisotropic yielding of granular media, creating fundamental locomotion challenges.

Method: Used hexapedal robot on tiltable granular bed to measure locomotion speed and slope-dependent granular forces; developed robot-terrain interaction model predicting anchoring timing, step length, and speed.

Result: Slope-induced performance loss primarily caused by delayed anchoring and increased backward slip rather than excessive sinkage; failure phase diagrams identify sinkage- and slippage-induced failure regimes.

Conclusion: Physics-informed framework provides predictive insight into terrain-dependent failure mechanisms and offers guidance for safer, more robust robot operation on deformable inclines.

Abstract: Locomotion on granular slopes such as sand dunes remains a fundamental challenge for legged robots due to reduced shear strength and gravity-induced anisotropic yielding of granular media. Using a hexapedal robot on a tiltable granular bed, we systematically measure locomotion speed together with slope-dependent normal and shear granular resistive forces. While normal penetration resistance remains nearly unchanged with inclination, shear resistance decreases substantially as slope angle increases. Guided by these measurements, we develop a simple robot-terrain interaction model that predicts anchoring timing, step length, and resulting robot speed, as functions of terrain strength and slope angle. The model reveals that slope-induced performance loss is primarily governed by delayed anchoring and increased backward slip rather than excessive sinkage. By extending the model to generalized terrain conditions, we construct failure phase diagrams that identify sinkage- and slippage-induced failure regimes, enabling quantitative risk estimation for locomotion on granular slopes. This physics-informed framework provides predictive insight into terrain-dependent failure mechanisms and offers guidance for safer and more robust robot operation on deformable inclines.

</details>


### [388] [Feasibility Restoration under Conflicting STL Specifications with Pareto-Optimal Refinement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.06947)
*Tianhao Wu,Yiwei Lyu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A two-stage framework for handling conflicting STL specifications in MPC: first restores feasibility via minimal relaxation, then refines solutions through multi-objective optimization with Pareto front analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: STL specifications can become conflicting in real-world robotics applications (safety rules, traffic regulations, task objectives), making traditional STL-constrained MPC infeasible and leading to conservative behaviors like freezing that increase risks in safety-critical scenarios.

Method: Two-stage framework: 1) Restores feasibility via minimal relaxation of conflicting STL specifications, 2) Formulates feasible solution as value-aware multi-objective optimization problem using ε-constraint method to approximate Pareto front for tradeoff analysis.

Result: The approach avoids deadlock under conflicting STL specifications and enables interpretable decision-making in safety-critical applications, demonstrated through an autonomous driving case study.

Conclusion: Proposed framework effectively handles conflicting STL specifications in MPC by combining feasibility restoration with multi-objective optimization, providing interpretable decision-making capabilities for safety-critical robotics applications.

Abstract: Signal Temporal Logic (STL) is expressive formal language that specifies spatio-temporal requirements in robotics. Its quantitative robustness semantics can be easily integrated with optimization-based control frameworks. However, STL specifications may become conflicting in real-world applications, where safety rules, traffic regulations, and task objectives can be cannot be satisfied together. In these situations, traditional STL-constrained Model Predictive Control (MPC) becomes infeasible and default to conservative behaviors such as freezing, which can largely increase risks in safety-critical scenarios. In this paper, we proposes a unified two-stage framework that first restores feasibility via minimal relaxation, then refine the feasible solution by formulating it as a value-aware multi-objective optimization problem. Using $\varepsilon$-constraint method, we approximate the Pareto front of the multi-objective optimization, which allows analysis of tradeoffs among competing objectives and counterfactual analysis of alternative actions. We demonstrate that the proposed approach avoids deadlock under conflicting STL specifications and enables interpretable decision-making in safety-critical applications by conducting a case study in autonomous driving.

</details>


### [389] [Is Your Safe Controller Actually Safe? A Critical Review of CBF Tautologies and Hidden Assumptions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.06954)
*Taekyung Kim*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Critical review of Control Barrier Functions (CBFs) in robotics, highlighting gaps between theory and practice, distinguishing candidate vs. valid CBFs, and providing practical guidelines for real safety guarantees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the recurring gap between theoretical CBF foundations and practical implementation in robotic safety, especially for systems with input constraints where mathematical assumptions don't match constructive realization.

Method: Critical analysis of CBF applications, distinction between candidate and valid CBFs, examination of system dynamics/actuation limits/class-K functions interplay, revisiting low-dimensional examples to show when CBFs work/fail, and developing practical guidelines.

Result: Identifies limitations in current CBF demonstrations (often limited to passively safe systems), shows when CBF formulations provide valid safety guarantees vs. fail due to misuse, and provides guidelines for constructing realizable safety arguments.

Conclusion: Bridges gap between theoretical guarantees and actual implementation through practical guidelines and interactive visualization, emphasizing need for constructive safety arguments beyond passive safety systems.

Abstract: This tutorial provides a critical review of the practical application of Control Barrier Functions (CBFs) in robotic safety. While the theoretical foundations of CBFs are well-established, I identify a recurring gap between the mathematical assumption of a safe controller's existence and its constructive realization in systems with input constraints. I highlight the distinction between candidate and valid CBFs by analyzing the interplay of system dynamics, actuation limits, and class-K functions. I further show that some purported demonstrations of safe robot policies or controllers are limited to passively safe systems, such as single integrators or kinematic manipulators, where safety is already inherited from the underlying physics and even naive geometric hard constraints suffice to prevent collisions. By revisiting simple low-dimensional examples, I show when CBF formulations provide valid safety guarantees and when they fail due to common misuses. I then provide practical guidelines for constructing realizable safety arguments for systems without such passive safety. The goal of this tutorial is to bridge the gap between theoretical guarantees and actual implementation, supported by an open-source interactive web demonstration that visualizes these concepts intuitively.

</details>


### [390] [Energy-Efficient Collaborative Transport of Tether-Suspended Payloads via Rotating Equilibrium](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.06955)
*Eric Foss,Andrew Tai,Carlo Bosio,Mark W. Mueller*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Rotating tethered quadrotor systems reduce power consumption by 20% compared to static lifting through centrifugal forces enabling vertical thrust.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current collaborative aerial transportation suffers from energy inefficiency due to horizontal thrust components needed for static equilibrium, limiting practical applications.

Method: Propose operating tethered flying systems at rotating equilibrium using steady circular motion to generate centrifugal forces, allowing purely vertical thrust from quadrotors.

Result: Rotating equilibria reduce power consumption by up to 20% compared to static lifting, enabling more efficient collaborative aerial transportation.

Conclusion: Rotating tethered systems offer significant energy savings and practical advantages over conventional static approaches for collaborative aerial payload transportation.

Abstract: Collaborative aerial transportation of tethered payloads is fundamentally limited by space, power, and weight constraints. Conventional approaches rely on static equilibrium conditions, where each vehicle tilts to generate the forces that ensure they maintain a formation geometry that avoids aerodynamic interactions and collision. This horizontal thrust component represents a significant energy penalty compared to the ideal case in which each vehicle produces purely vertical thrust to lift the payload. Operating in tighter tether configurations can minimize this effect, but at the cost of either having to fly the vehicles in closer proximity, which risks collision, or significantly increasing the length of the tether, which increases complexity and reduces potential use-cases. We propose operating the tether-suspended flying system at a rotating equilibrium. By maintaining steady circular motion, centrifugal forces provide the necessary horizontal tether tension, allowing each quadrotor to generate purely vertical thrust and thus reducing the total force (and power) required compared to an equilibrium where the thrusts are not vertical. It also allows for a wider range of tether configurations to be used without sacrificing efficiency. Results demonstrate that rotating equilibria can reduce power consumption relative to static lifting by up to 20%, making collaborative aerial solutions more practically relevant.

</details>


### [391] [VSL-Skin: Individually Addressable Phase-Change Voxel Skin for Variable-Stiffness and Virtual Joints Bridging Soft and Rigid Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.06979)
*Zihan Oliver Zeng,Jiajun An,Preston Luk,Upinder Kaur*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: VSL-Skin enables voxel-level stiffness control with 30% axial compression, self-repair, and virtual joints for reconfigurable robotics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Soft robots lack load-bearing capability while rigid robots lack adaptability; existing variable-stiffness systems operate at coarse scales limiting precise stiffness control and joint placement.

Method: Variable Stiffness Lattice Skin (VSL-Skin) with individually addressable voxels using phase-change materials, enabling centimeter-scale stiffness modulation, selective voxel activation for virtual joints, and thermal cycling for self-repair.

Result: Achieves 30% axial compression, 75-second thermal switching cycles, two orders of magnitude stiffness modulation across multiple modes, and cut-to-fit integration preserving addressability.

Conclusion: VSL-Skin establishes morphological intelligence as engineerable property, enabling platform-agnostic deployment and advancing autonomous reconfigurable robotics with precise stiffness control.

Abstract: Soft robots are compliant but often cannot support loads or hold their shape, while rigid robots provide structural strength but are less adaptable. Existing variable-stiffness systems usually operate at the scale of whole segments or patches, which limits precise control over stiffness distribution and virtual joint placement. This paper presents the Variable Stiffness Lattice Skin (VSL-Skin), the first system to enable individually addressable voxel-level morphological control with centimeter-scale precision. The system provides three main capabilities: nearly two orders of magnitude stiffness modulation across axial (15-1200 N/mm), shear (45-850 N/mm), bending (8*10^2 - 3*10^4 N/deg), and torsional modes with centimeter-scale spatial control; the first demonstrated 30% axial compression in phase-change systems while maintaining structural integrity; and autonomous component-level self-repair through thermal cycling, which eliminates fatigue accumulation and enables programmable sacrificial joints for predictable failure management. Selective voxel activation creates six canonical virtual joint types with programmable compliance while preserving structural integrity in non-activated regions. The platform incorporates closed-form design models and finite element analysis for predictive synthesis of stiffness patterns and joint placement. Experimental validation demonstrates 30% axial contraction, thermal switching in 75-second cycles, and cut-to-fit integration that preserves addressability after trimming. The row-column architecture enables platform-agnostic deployment across diverse robotic systems without specialized infrastructure. This framework establishes morphological intelligence as an engineerable system property and advances autonomous reconfigurable robotics.

</details>


### [392] [Foundational World Models Accurately Detect Bimanual Manipulator Failures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.06987)
*Isaac R. Ward,Michelle Ho,Houjun Liu,Aaron Feldman,Joseph Vincent,Liam Kruse,Sean Cheong,Duncan Eddy,Mykel J. Kochenderfer,Mac Schwager*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A conformal prediction-based runtime monitor using world models in compressed latent spaces detects failures in bimanual manipulation with high accuracy and low parameter count.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deploying visuomotor robots at scale is challenging due to potential anomalous failures that could degrade performance, cause damage, or endanger human life. Bimanual manipulators have vast state spaces (high-dimensional images + proprioceptive signals) where explicitly defining failure modes is infeasible.

Method: Train a probabilistic, history-informed world model within the compressed latent space of a pretrained vision foundation model (NVIDIA's Cosmos Tokenizer). Use model uncertainty estimates as non-conformity scores within a conformal prediction framework to develop a runtime monitor that correlates high uncertainty with anomalous failures.

Result: Approach outperforms statistical techniques and learning-based baselines, requiring ~1/20th of trainable parameters of next-best learning approach while achieving 3.8% higher failure detection rate. Tested on simulated Push-T environment and new Bimanual Cable Manipulation dataset.

Conclusion: The method enables safe deployment of manipulator robots in real-world environments where reliability is non-negotiable, using efficient uncertainty estimation through conformal prediction in compressed latent spaces.

Abstract: Deploying visuomotor robots at scale is challenging due to the potential for anomalous failures to degrade performance, cause damage, or endanger human life. Bimanual manipulators are no exception; these robots have vast state spaces comprised of high-dimensional images and proprioceptive signals. Explicitly defining failure modes within such state spaces is infeasible. In this work, we overcome these challenges by training a probabilistic, history informed, world model within the compressed latent space of a pretrained vision foundation model (NVIDIA's Cosmos Tokenizer). The model outputs uncertainty estimates alongside its predictions that serve as non-conformity scores within a conformal prediction framework. We use these scores to develop a runtime monitor, correlating periods of high uncertainty with anomalous failures. To test these methods, we use the simulated Push-T environment and the Bimanual Cable Manipulation dataset, the latter of which we introduce in this work. This new dataset features trajectories with multiple synchronized camera views, proprioceptive signals, and annotated failures from a challenging data center maintenance task. We benchmark our methods against baselines from the anomaly detection and out-of-distribution detection literature, and show that our approach considerably outperforms statistical techniques. Furthermore, we show that our approach requires approximately one twentieth of the trainable parameters as the next-best learning-based approach, yet outperforms it by 3.8% in terms of failure detection rate, paving the way toward safely deploying manipulator robots in real-world environments where reliability is non-negotiable.

</details>


### [393] [Two-Stage Path Following for Mobile Manipulators via Dimensionality-Reduced Graph Search and Numerical Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.07003)
*Fuyu Guo,Yuting Mei,Yuyao Zhang,Qian Tang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Two-stage planning framework decouples 8-DoF mobile manipulator path following into tractable 2-DoF base optimization with continuous refinement for smooth, reachable trajectories.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mobile manipulators face challenges in efficient path following due to high-dimensional configuration spaces and kinematic constraints, requiring robust planning methods that balance computational efficiency with precision.

Method: Two-stage framework: 1) IRM discretizes task-space path into multi-layer graph, Dijkstra-based DP finds initial feasible path; 2) Convex hull transformation of feasible base regions enables L-BFGS continuous refinement for smoothness while enforcing reachability constraints.

Result: Achieves sub-millimeter kinematic accuracy in simulation, and physical experiments on omnidirectional mobile manipulator validate robustness and practical applicability.

Conclusion: The proposed decoupled approach effectively solves 8-DoF path following by reducing to tractable 2-DoF optimization with continuous refinement, demonstrating both theoretical precision and practical robustness.

Abstract: Efficient path following for mobile manipulators is often hindered by high-dimensional configuration spaces and kinematic constraints. This paper presents a robust two-stage configuration planning framework that decouples the 8-DoF planning problem into a tractable 2-DoF base optimization under a yaw-fixed base planning assumption. In the first stage, the proposed approach utilizes IRM to discretize the task-space path into a multi-layer graph, where an initial feasible path is extracted via a Dijkstra-based dynamic programming approach to ensure computational efficiency and global optimality within the discretized graph. In the second stage, to overcome discrete search quantization, feasible base regions are transformed into convex hulls, enabling subsequent continuous refinement via the L-BFGS algorithm to maximize trajectory smoothness while strictly enforcing reachability constraints. Simulation results demonstrate the theoretical precision of the proposed method by achieving sub-millimeter kinematic accuracy in simulation, and physical experiments on an omnidirectional mobile manipulator further validate the framework's robustness and practical applicability.

</details>


### [394] [SSP: Safety-guaranteed Surgical Policy via Joint Optimization of Behavioral and Spatial Constraints](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.07032)
*Jianshu Hu,ZhiYuan Guan,Lei Song,Kantaphat Leelakunwet,Hesheng Wang,Wei Xiao,Qi Dou,Yutong Ban*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SSP framework combines Neural ODEs for uncertainty-aware dynamics modeling with Control Barrier Functions to provide formal safety guarantees for surgical robot policies while maintaining task performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Data-driven surgical robot policies (RL/IL) lack formal safety guarantees needed for clinical deployment, creating a gap between data-driven generality and safety requirements.

Method: Uses Neural ODEs to learn uncertainty-aware dynamics from demonstrations, then applies robust Control Barrier Functions to minimally modify policy actions to enforce behavioral and spatial constraints.

Result: Achieves near-zero constraint violation rates while maintaining high task success rates in both SurRoL simulation and da Vinci Research Kit (dVRK) validation.

Conclusion: SSP framework successfully bridges the gap between data-driven surgical autonomy and formal safety guarantees, enabling safer clinical deployment of learned policies.

Abstract: The paradigm of robot-assisted surgery is shifting toward data-driven autonomy, where policies learned via Reinforcement Learning (RL) or Imitation Learning (IL) enable the execution of complex tasks. However, these ``black-box" policies often lack formal safety guarantees, a critical requirement for clinical deployment. In this paper, we propose the Safety-guaranteed Surgical Policy (SSP) framework to bridge the gap between data-driven generality and formal safety. We utilize Neural Ordinary Differential Equations (Neural ODEs) to learn an uncertainty-aware dynamics model from demonstration data. This learned model underpins a robust Control Barrier Function (CBF) safety controller, which minimally alters the actions of a surgical policy to ensure strict safety under uncertainty. Our controller enforces two constraint categories: behavioral constraints (restricting the task space of the agent) and spatial constraints (defining surgical no-go zones). We instantiate the SSP framework with surgical policies derived from RL, IL and Control Lyapunov Functions (CLF). Validation on in both the SurRoL simulation and da Vinci Research Kit (dVRK) demonstrates that our method achieves a near-zero constraint violation rate while maintaining high task success rates compared to unconstrained baselines.

</details>


### [395] [TacDexGrasp: Compliant and Robust Dexterous Grasping with Tactile Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.07040)
*Yubin Ke,Jiayi Chen,Hang Lv,Xiao Zhou,He Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SOCP-based tactile controller prevents rotational slip in multi-fingered grasping by constraining tangential-to-normal force ratios below friction coefficients.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-fingered hands have high potential for compliant grasping but face challenges with force distribution and rotational slip when grasping away from object's center of mass.

Method: Uses tactile feedback and Second-Order Cone Programming (SOCP) controller without explicit torque modeling or slip detection. Key insights: rotational slip induces translational slip at some contacts, and tangential-to-normal force ratio serves as early stability indicator.

Result: Real-world experiments on 12 diverse objects demonstrate robust and compliant grasping performance against both translational and rotational slip.

Conclusion: The SOCP-based tactile controller effectively maintains grasp stability by actively constraining force ratios, enabling robust multi-fingered grasping without complex slip detection or torque modeling.

Abstract: Multi-fingered hands offer great potential for compliant and robust grasping of unknown objects, yet their high-dimensional force control presents a significant challenge. This work addresses two key problems: (1) distributing forces across multiple contacts to counteract an object's weight, and (2) preventing rotational slip caused by gravitational torque when a grasp is distant from the object's center of mass. We address these challenges via tactile feedback and a Second-Order Cone Programming (SOCP)-based controller, without explicit torque modeling or slip detection. Our key insights are (1) rotational slip inevitably induces translational slip at some contact points for a multi-fingered grasp, and (2) the ratio of tangential to normal force at each contact is an effective early stability indicator. By actively constraining this ratio for each finger below the estimated friction coefficient, our controller maintains grasp stability against both translational and rotational slip. Real-world experiments on 12 diverse objects demonstrate the robustness and compliance of our approach.

</details>


### [396] [GuideTWSI: A Diverse Tactile Walking Surface Indicator Dataset from Synthetic and Real-World Images for Blind and Low-Vision Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.07060)
*Hochul Hwang,Soowan Yang,Anh N. H. Nguyen,Parth Goel,Krisha Adhikari,Sunghoon I. Lee,Joydeep Biswas,Nicholas A. Giudice,Donghyun Kim*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: TWSIs are critical for BLV navigation but underrepresented in datasets, especially truncated domes used in North America/Europe, causing poor model generalization and safety risks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: TWSIs are safety-critical landmarks for BLV pedestrians, but existing datasets lack robot-relevant viewpoints and are geographically biased toward East Asian directional bars, overlooking truncated domes used in North America/Europe, leading to poor model generalization and safety risks.

Method: The paper conducted observation sessions with BLV guide dog handlers, trainers, and an O&M specialist to confirm the importance of reliable TWSI segmentation, and identified gaps in existing datasets.

Result: Found that models trained only on bar-centric data struggle to generalize to dome-based warnings, leading to missed detections and false stops in safety-critical environments.

Conclusion: There is a critical need for large-scale, geographically diverse TWSI datasets with robot-relevant viewpoints to improve navigation assistance for BLV individuals.

Abstract: Tactile Walking Surface Indicators (TWSIs) are safety-critical landmarks that blind and low-vision (BLV) pedestrians use to locate crossings and hazard zones. From our observation sessions with BLV guide dog handlers, trainers, and an O&M specialist, we confirmed the critical importance of reliable and accurate TWSI segmentation for navigation assistance of BLV individuals. Achieving such reliability requires large-scale annotated data. However, TWSIs are severely underrepresented in existing urban perception datasets, and even existing dedicated paving datasets are limited: they lack robot-relevant viewpoints (e.g., egocentric or top-down) and are geographically biased toward East Asian directional bars - raised parallel strips used for continuous guidance along sidewalks. This narrow focus overlooks truncated domes - rows of round bumps used primarily in North America and Europe as detectable warnings at curbs, crossings, and platform edges. As a result, models trained only on bar-centric data struggle to generalize to dome-based warnings, leading to missed detections and false stops in safety-critical environments.

</details>


### [397] [Morphology-Independent Facial Expression Imitation for Human-Face Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.07068)
*Xu Chen,Rui Gao,Che Sun,Zhehang Liu,Yuwei Wu,Shuo Yang,Yunde Jia*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A morphology-independent expression imitation method for human-face robots that decouples expressions from facial morphology to produce more realistic expressions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for facial expression imitation on human-face robots are susceptible to interference from facial morphology, leading to performance drops. Current approaches that map 2D facial landmarks to robot actuator commands don't effectively separate expression from morphology.

Method: Proposes a two-module approach: 1) Expression decoupling module that learns expression semantics by disentangling expression representation from morphology representation in self-supervised manner, 2) Expression transfer module that maps representations to robot actuator commands using learning objective of perceiving expression errors.

Result: Developed custom human-face robot "Pengrui" as experimental platform. Extensive experiments demonstrate the method enables human-face robots to reproduce wide range of human-like expressions effectively.

Conclusion: The proposed morphology-independent expression imitation method successfully decouples expressions from facial morphology, eliminating morphological influence and producing more realistic expressions for human-face robots, advancing natural human-robot interaction.

Abstract: Accurate facial expression imitation on human-face robots is crucial for achieving natural human-robot interaction. Most existing methods have achieved photorealistic expression imitation through mapping 2D facial landmarks to a robot's actuator commands. Their imitation of landmark trajectories is susceptible to interference from facial morphology, which would lead to a performance drop. In this paper, we propose a morphology-independent expression imitation method that decouples expressions from facial morphology to eliminate morphological influence and produce more realistic expressions for human-face robots. Specifically, we construct an expression decoupling module to learn expression semantics by disentangling the expression representation from the morphology representation in a self-supervised manner. We devise an expression transfer module to map the representations to the robot's actuator commands through a learning objective of perceiving expression errors, producing accurate facial expressions based on the learned expression semantics. To support experimental validation, a custom-designed and highly expressive human-face robot, namely Pengrui, is developed to serve as an experimental platform for realistic expression imitation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method enables the human-face robot to reproduce a wide range of human-like expressions effectively. All code and implementation details of the robot will be released.

</details>


### [398] [The Talking Robot: Distortion-Robust Acoustic Models for Robot-Robot Communication](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.07072)
*Hanlong Li,Karishma Kamalahasan,Jiahui Li,Kazuhiro Nakadai,Shreyas Kousik*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Artoo is a lightweight neural acoustic communication system for robots that uses co-trained TTS transmitter and ASR receiver networks to achieve robust robot-to-robot communication with minimal parameters and fast inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional acoustic communication systems use hand-designed signal processing, but robot-to-robot communication doesn't need to preserve human speech characteristics like timbre or prosody - only decoding accuracy under channel distortion matters. This allows for more efficient, specialized communication systems.

Method: The system pairs a lightweight text-to-speech transmitter (1.18M parameters) with a conformer-based automatic speech recognition receiver (938K parameters), jointly optimized through a differentiable channel. A three-phase co-training curriculum teaches the transmitter to produce distortion-robust acoustic encodings.

Result: Achieves 8.3% character error rate at 0 dB SNR, surpassing baseline performance under noise. The entire system requires only 2.1M parameters (8.4 MB) and runs in under 13 ms end-to-end on a CPU.

Conclusion: Artoo demonstrates that learned acoustic communication systems can be highly efficient and robust for robot-to-robot communication, with minimal computational requirements suitable for resource-constrained robotic platforms.

Abstract: We present Artoo, a learned acoustic communication system for robots that replaces hand-designed signal processing with end-to-end co-trained neural networks. Our system pairs a lightweight text-to-speech (TTS) transmitter (1.18M parameters) with a conformer-based automatic speech recognition (ASR) receiver (938K parameters), jointly optimized through a differentiable channel. Unlike human speech, robot-to-robot communication is paralinguistics-free: the system need not preserve timbre, prosody, or naturalness, only maximize decoding accuracy under channel distortion. Through a three-phase co-training curriculum, the TTS transmitter learns to produce distortion-robust acoustic encodings that surpass the baseline under noise, achieving 8.3% CER at 0 dB SNR. The entire system requires only 2.1M parameters (8.4 MB) and runs in under 13 ms end-to-end on a CPU, making it suitable for deployment on resource-constrained robotic platforms.

</details>


### [399] [VLN-Cache: Enabling Token Caching for VLN Models with Visual/Semantic Dynamics Awareness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.07080)
*Zihao Zheng,Zhihao Mao,Xingyue Zhou,Jiayu Chen,Maoliang Li,Xinhao Sun,Hailong Zou,Zhaobo Zhang,Xuanzhe Liu,Donggang Cao,Hong Mei,Xiang Chen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: VLN-Cache: A training-free caching framework for Vision-and-Language Navigation that addresses visual and semantic dynamics through view-aligned remapping and task-relevance filtering, achieving 1.52x speedup while maintaining navigation performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large vision-language models enable VLN but have high inference costs that conflict with real-time deployment. Existing token caching methods fail in VLN due to dynamic camera viewpoints and shifting semantic focus during navigation.

Method: VLN-Cache introduces: 1) view-aligned remapping to recover geometric correspondences when camera viewpoints shift, 2) task-relevance saliency filter to veto reuse at semantic transitions, and 3) layer-adaptive entropy policy to balance per-layer reuse budget.

Result: Experiments on R2R-CE simulation benchmark show up to 1.52x speedup while maintaining competitive navigation success rates compared to baseline methods.

Conclusion: VLN-Cache effectively addresses the unique challenges of token caching in dynamic navigation environments, enabling efficient real-time VLN deployment without compromising navigation performance.

Abstract: Vision-and-Language Navigation (VLN) increasingly relies on large vision-language models, but their inference cost conflicts with real-time deployment. Token caching is a promising training-free strategy that avoids redundant computation by reusing stable visual tokens across frames. However, existing methods assume a static camera and fixed semantic focus, assumptions that VLN fundamentally violates. We identify two failure modes: (1) visual dynamics, where viewpoint shift displaces token positions across frames, causing position-wise matching to pair misaligned content; (2) semantic dynamics, where token relevance shifts across task stages as navigation progresses, making cached states stale. We propose VLN-Cache, a visual-dynamic-aware and semantic-dynamic-aware caching framework that introduces view-aligned remapping to recover geometric correspondences and a task-relevance saliency filter to veto reuse at semantic transitions. A layer-adaptive entropy policy further balances the per-layer reuse budget. Experiments on the R2R-CE simulation benchmark show up to 1.52x speedup while maintaining competitive navigation success rates.

</details>


### [400] [ACLM: ADMM-Based Distributed Model Predictive Control for Collaborative Loco-Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.07095)
*Ziyi Zhou,Pengyuan Shu,Ruize Cao,Yuntian Zhao,Ye Zhao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ADMM-based distributed MPC for collaborative loco-manipulation with quadruped robots, enabling scalable real-time planning while maintaining dynamic coupling awareness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need for collaborative heavy payload transport in unstructured environments, with limitations in existing approaches: centralized planning doesn't scale, while decentralized methods ignore dynamic interactions.

Method: ADMM-based distributed MPC framework that decomposes global optimal control into parallel robot-level subproblems with consensus constraints, plus wrench-aware whole-body controller for execution.

Result: Demonstrated scalability up to 4 robots, real-time performance with fast ADMM convergence, and robustness to model uncertainty in extensive simulations.

Conclusion: Proposed framework successfully balances scalability and dynamic coupling awareness for collaborative loco-manipulation, enabling real-time performance for multi-robot teams.

Abstract: Collaborative transportation of heavy payloads via loco-manipulation is a challenging yet essential capability for legged robots operating in complex, unstructured environments. Centralized planning methods, e.g., holistic trajectory optimization, capture dynamic coupling among robots and payloads but scale poorly with system size, limiting real-time applicability. In contrast, hierarchical and fully decentralized approaches often neglect force and dynamic interactions, leading to conservative behavior. This study proposes an Alternating Direction Method of Multipliers (ADMM)-based distributed model predictive control framework for collaborative loco-manipulation with a team of quadruped robots with manipulators. By exploiting the payload-induced coupling structure, the global optimal control problem is decomposed into parallel individual-robot-level subproblems with consensus constraints. The distributed planner operates in a receding-horizon fashion and achieves fast convergence, requiring only a few ADMM iterations per planning cycle. A wrench-aware whole-body controller executes the planned trajectories, tracking both motion and interaction wrenches. Extensive simulations with up to four robots demonstrate scalability, real-time performance, and robustness to model uncertainty.

</details>


### [401] [Towards Scalable Probabilistic Human Motion Prediction with Gaussian Processes for Safe Human-Robot Collaboration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.07096)
*Jinger Chong,Xiaotong Zhang,Kamal Youcef-Toumi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A scalable Gaussian Process framework for full-body human motion prediction with reliable uncertainty calibration, achieving competitive accuracy with 8x fewer parameters than deep learning methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Safe human-robot collaboration requires accurate human motion prediction with well-calibrated uncertainty for real-time robot anticipation and reaction.

Method: Structured multitask variational Gaussian Process framework with temporal correlations, joint-dimension-level factorization for scalability, and continuous 6D rotation representation for kinematic consistency.

Result: Achieves 50% lower KDE NLL than baselines, 0.021m mean CRPS, 3-18% higher MAE than deep learning methods, with conservative uncertainty calibration and only 0.24-0.35M parameters (8x fewer than comparable approaches).

Conclusion: Scalable GP-based models can deliver competitive accuracy with reliable, interpretable uncertainty estimates suitable for real-time robotics applications like motion planning and collision avoidance.

Abstract: Accurate human motion prediction with well-calibrated uncertainty is critical for safe human-robot collaboration (HRC), where robots must anticipate and react to human movements in real time. We propose a structured multitask variational Gaussian Process (GP) framework for full-body human motion prediction that captures temporal correlations and leverages joint-dimension-level factorization for scalability, while using a continuous 6D rotation representation to preserve kinematic consistency. Evaluated on Human3.6M (H3.6M), our model achieves up to 50 lower kernel density estimate negative log-likelihood (KDE NLL) than strong baselines, a mean continuous ranked probability score (CRPS) of 0.021 m, and deterministic mean angle error (MAE) that is 3-18% higher than competitive deep learning methods. Empirical coverage analysis shows that the fraction of ground-truth outcomes contained within predicted confidence intervals gradually decreases with horizon, remaining conservative for lower-confidence intervals and near-nominal for higher-confidence intervals, with only modest calibration drift at longer horizons. Despite its probabilistic formulation, our model requires only 0.24-0.35 M parameters, roughly eight times fewer than comparable approaches, and exhibits modest inference times, indicating suitability for real-time deployment. Extensive ablation studies further validated the choice of 6D rotation representation and Matern 3/2 + Linear kernel, and guided the selection of the number of inducing points and latent dimensionality. These results demonstrate that scalable GP-based models can deliver competitive accuracy together with reliable and interpretable uncertainty estimates for downstream robotics tasks such as motion planning and collision avoidance.

</details>


### [402] [Learning From Failures: Efficient Reinforcement Learning Control with Episodic Memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.07110)
*Chenyang Miao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: FEMA uses episodic memory to store and recall failure experiences, preventing robots from repeating unstable states and improving RL sample efficiency by 33.11% on MuJoCo tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Early-stage RL training for robots is dominated by premature terminations (collisions/falls), leading to short-horizon, low-return trajectories that hinder convergence and limit long-horizon exploration.

Method: FEMA (Failure Episodic Memory Alert) stores short-horizon failure experiences in an episodic memory module, retrieves similar failures during interactions to prevent recurrent relapses into unstable states, and guides policy toward long-horizon trajectories.

Result: 33.11% sample-efficiency improvement on MuJoCo tasks across several classical RL algorithms, and effective integration into parallelized PPO training for real-world bipedal robot tasks.

Conclusion: FEMA effectively addresses premature termination issues in robot RL by leveraging failure memory to improve exploration and sample efficiency, demonstrating practical value in both simulation and real-world applications.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning has achieved remarkable success in robot learning. However, under challenging exploration and contact-rich dynamics, early-stage training is frequently dominated by premature terminations such as collisions and falls. As a result, learning is overwhelmed by short-horizon, low-return trajectories, which hinder convergence and limit long-horizon exploration. To alleviate this issue, we propose a technique called Failure Episodic Memory Alert (FEMA). FEMA explicitly stores short-horizon failure experiences through an episodic memory module. During interactions, it retrieves similar failure experiences and prevents the robot from recurrently relapsing into unstable states, guiding the policy toward long-horizon trajectories with greater long-term value. FEMA can be combined easily with model-free reinforcement learning algorithms, and yields a substantial sample-efficiency improvement of 33.11% on MuJoCo tasks across several classical RL algorithms. Furthermore, integrating FEMA into a parallelized PPO training pipeline demonstrates its effectiveness on a real-world bipedal robot task.

</details>


### [403] [Efficient Trajectory Optimization for Autonomous Racing via Formula-1 Data-Driven Initialization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.07126)
*Samir Shehadeh,Lukas Kutsch,Nils Dengler,Sicong Pan,Maren Bennewitz*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Learning-informed trajectory initialization using Formula 1 telemetry accelerates racing optimization convergence while preserving final lap times.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional trajectory optimization for autonomous racing is sensitive to initialization and may converge slowly or to suboptimal solutions when using heuristic trajectories like centerline or minimum-curvature paths.

Method: Constructed multi-track Formula 1 trajectory dataset from noisy GPS telemetry aligned to standardized reference lines across 17 tracks. Developed neural network that predicts expert-like raceline offset directly from local track geometry without explicit vehicle dynamics modeling. Used predicted raceline as informed seed for minimum-time optimal control solver.

Result: Experiments on all 17 tracks show learned initialization accelerates solver convergence and significantly reduces runtime compared to traditional geometric baselines while preserving final optimized lap time.

Conclusion: Expert driving behavior can be effectively leveraged as an initialization prior to improve trajectory optimization efficiency in autonomous racing applications.

Abstract: Trajectory optimization is a central component of fast and efficient autonomous racing. However practical optimization pipelines remain highly sensitive to initialization and may converge slowly or to suboptimal local solutions when seeded with heuristic trajectories such as the centerline or minimum-curvature paths. To address this limitation, we leverage expert driving behavior as a initialization prior and propose a learning-informed initialization strategy based on real-world Formula 1 telemetry. To this end, we first construct a multi-track Formula~1 trajectory dataset by reconstructing and aligning noisy GPS telemetry to a standardized reference-line representation across 17 tracks. Building on this, we present a neural network that predicts an expert-like raceline offset directly from local track geometry, without explicitly modeling vehicle dynamics or forces. The predicted raceline is then used as an informed seed for a minimum-time optimal control solver. Experiments on all 17 tracks demonstrate that the learned initialization accelerates solver convergence and significantly reduces runtime compared to traditional geometric baselines, while preserving the final optimized lap time.

</details>


### [404] [DexKnot: Generalizable Visuomotor Policy Learning for Dexterous Bag-Knotting Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.07136)
*Jiayuan Zhang,Ruihai Wu,Haojun Chen,Yuran Wang,Yifan Zhong,Ceyao Zhang,Yaodong Yang,Yuanpei Chen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: DexKnot combines keypoint affordance with diffusion policy to enable robots to reliably knot plastic bags across unseen instances and deformations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Knotting plastic bags is challenging for robots due to infinite degrees of freedom and complex dynamics, and existing methods struggle with generalization to unseen bag instances or deformations.

Method: Learns shape-agnostic bag representation from keypoint correspondence data collected through real-world manual deformation, identifies keypoints for unseen configurations via matching to reference, then uses diffusion transformer to generate robot actions from human demonstrations.

Result: DexKnot achieves reliable and consistent knotting performance across a variety of previously unseen bag instances and deformations.

Conclusion: The framework enables effective policy generalization by reducing observation space dimensionality to sparse keypoints, combining keypoint affordance with diffusion policy for robust bag-knotting.

Abstract: Knotting plastic bags is a common task in daily life, yet it is challenging for robots due to the bags' infinite degrees of freedom and complex physical dynamics. Existing methods often struggle in generalization to unseen bag instances or deformations. To address this, we present DexKnot, a framework that combines keypoint affordance with diffusion policy to learn a generalizable bag-knotting policy. Our approach learns a shape-agnostic representation of bags from keypoint correspondence data collected through real-world manual deformation. For an unseen bag configuration, the keypoints can be identified by matching the representation to a reference. These keypoints are then provided to a diffusion transformer, which generates robot action based on a small number of human demonstrations. DexKnot enables effective policy generalization by reducing the dimensionality of observation space into a sparse set of keypoints. Experiments show that DexKnot achieves reliable and consistent knotting performance across a variety of previously unseen instances and deformations.

</details>


### [405] [Model-based thermal drift compensation for high-precision hexapod robot actuators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.07141)
*Clément Robert,Alain Vissiere,Olivier Company,Pierre Noire,Thierry Roux,Sébastien Krut*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposed method reduces thermal expansion errors in hexapod robots by 80+% using temperature-based modeling of actuator expansion


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Thermal expansion from internal/external heat sources causes positioning errors in high-precision hexapod robots, reducing accuracy and repeatability

Method: Develop theoretical model linking actuator expansion to surface temperatures, then experimentally calibrate coefficients using interferometric measurements on test bench

Result: Experimental validation shows >80% reduction in thermally induced expansion, enabling effective thermal drift correction

Conclusion: Method successfully corrects thermal drift in hexapod actuators, paving way for full robot thermal compensation and similar robotics applications

Abstract: Thermal expansion is a significant source of positioning error in high-precision hexapod robots (Gough-Stewart platforms). Any variation in the temperature of the hexapod's parts induces expansion, which alters their kinematic model and reduces the robot's accuracy and repeatability. These variations may arise from internal heat sources (such as motors, encoders, and electronics) or from environmental changes. In this study, a method is proposed to anticipate and therefore correct the thermal drift of one of the hexapod precision electro-mechanical actuators. This method is based on determining a model that links the expansion state of the actuator at any given moment to the temperature of some well-chosen points on its surface. This model was initially developed theoretically. Its coefficients were then adjusted experimentally on a specific test-bench, based on a rigorous measurement campaign of actuator expansion using a high-precision interferometric measurement system. Experimental validation demonstrates a reduction of thermally induced expansion by more than 80%. This paves the way for thermal drift correction across the entire robot or similar robotics parts.

</details>


### [406] [Tutorial on Aided Inertial Navigation Systems: A Modern Treatment Using Lie-Group Theoretical Methods](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.07143)
*Soulaimane Berkane*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Tutorial on control-oriented aided inertial navigation using Lie-group formulation on SE_2(3), focusing on geometric framework for fusing inertial measurements with aiding information while emphasizing invariance and symmetry principles.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a clear, implementation-oriented geometric framework for aided inertial navigation that makes invariance and symmetry principles explicit, bridging theory and practical application.

Method: Lie-group formulation centered on extended Special Euclidean group SE_2(3), with discussion of recent extensions including higher-order state representations, synchronous observer designs, and equivariant filtering methods.

Result: A coherent system-theoretic perspective that supports both understanding and practical use of modern aided inertial navigation methods, presented as a tutorial for readers.

Conclusion: The Lie-group approach provides a natural framework for aided inertial navigation, with recent extensions demonstrating the versatility of invariance and symmetry principles in developing practical navigation solutions.

Abstract: This tutorial presents a control-oriented introduction to aided inertial navigation systems using a Lie-group formulation centered on the extended Special Euclidean group SE_2(3). The focus is on developing a clear and implementation-oriented geometric framework for fusing inertial measurements with aiding information, while making the role of invariance and symmetry explicit. Recent extensions, including higher-order state representations, synchronous observer designs, and equivariant filtering methods, are discussed as natural continuations of the same underlying principles. The goal is to provide readers with a coherent system-theoretic perspective that supports both understanding and practical use of modern aided inertial navigation methods.

</details>


### [407] [RoTri-Diff: A Spatial Robot-Object Triadic Interaction-Guided Diffusion Model for Bimanual Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.07165)
*Zixuan Chen,Nga Teng Chan,Yiwen Hou,Chenrui Tie,Zixuan Liu,Haonan Chen,Junting Chen,Jieqi Shi,Yang Gao,Jing Huo,Lin Shao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: RoTri-Diff: A diffusion-based imitation learning framework that models Robot-Object Triadic Interactions for bimanual manipulation, improving coordination and reducing collisions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing imitation learning approaches for bimanual manipulation overlook dynamic geometric relationships between arms and objects, leading to collisions, unstable grasps, and degraded performance in complex tasks.

Method: Introduces RoTri representation encoding relative 6D poses between two arms and object to capture spatial triadic relationships. Develops RoTri-Diff, a diffusion-based framework combining RoTri constraints with robot keyposes and object motion in hierarchical diffusion process.

Result: Outperforms state-of-the-art baselines by 10.2% on 11 RLBench2 tasks and achieves stable performance on 4 challenging real-world bimanual tasks.

Conclusion: Explicit modeling of triadic interactions between arms and object enables generation of stable, coordinated trajectories for robust bimanual manipulation across different task modes.

Abstract: Bimanual manipulation is a fundamental robotic skill that requires continuous and precise coordination between two arms. While imitation learning (IL) is the dominant paradigm for acquiring this capability, existing approaches, whether robot-centric or object-centric, often overlook the dynamic geometric relationship among the two arms and the manipulated object. This limitation frequently leads to inter-arm collisions, unstable grasps, and degraded performance in complex tasks. To address this, in this paper we explicitly models the Robot-Object Triadic Interaction (RoTri) representation in bimanual systems, by encoding the relative 6D poses between the two arms and the object to capture their spatial triadic relationship and establish continuous triangular geometric constraints. Building on this, we further introduce RoTri-Diff, a diffusion-based imitation learning framework that combines RoTri constraints with robot keyposes and object motion in a hierarchical diffusion process. This enables the generation of stable, coordinated trajectories and robust execution across different modes of bimanual manipulation. Extensive experiments show that our approach outperforms state-of-the-art baselines by 10.2% on 11 representative RLBench2 tasks and achieves stable performance on 4 challenging real-world bimanual tasks. Project website: https://rotri-diff.github.io/.

</details>


### [408] [Vision-Guided MPPI for Agile Drone Racing: Navigating Arbitrary Gate Poses via Neural Signed Distance Fields](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.07199)
*Fangguo Zhao,Hanbing Zhang,Zhouheng Li,Xin Guan,Shuo Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Vision-guided MPPI control with neural signed distance field (Gate-SDF) enables reference-free agile drone racing through arbitrary gates without explicit gate pose estimation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current approaches for autonomous drone racing are brittle to spatial perturbations and track changes (relying on precomputed trajectories or explicit gate pose estimation), while end-to-end learning policies overfit to specific tracks and fail at zero-shot generalization.

Method: Propose Gate-SDF, a neural signed distance field that processes raw depth images to predict continuous spatial fields for collision repulsion and geometric guidance. Integrate this with sampling-based Model Predictive Path Integral (MPPI) controller, leveraging GPU parallelism to evaluate thousands of trajectory rollouts in real-time.

Result: The system achieves high-speed agile flight and successfully navigates unseen tracks with severe unmodeled gate displacements and orientation perturbations in both simulations and real-world experiments.

Conclusion: The vision-guided optimal control framework with Gate-SDF representation enables robust, reference-free agile drone racing that generalizes to unseen track layouts and handles spatial perturbations without explicit gate pose estimation.

Abstract: Autonomous drone racing requires the tight coupling of perception, planning, and control under extreme agility. However, recent approaches typically rely on precomputed spatial reference trajectories or explicit 6-DoF gate pose estimation, rendering them brittle to spatial perturbations, unmodeled track changes, and sensor noise. Conversely, end-to-end learning policies frequently overfit to specific track layouts and struggle with zero-shot generalization. To address these fundamental limitations, we propose a fully onboard, vision guided optimal control framework that enables reference-free agile flight through arbitrarily placed and oriented gates. Central to our approach is Gate-SDF, a novel, implicitly learned neural signed distance field. Gate-SDF directly processes raw, noisy depth images to predict a continuous spatial field that provides both collision repulsion and active geometric guidance toward the valid traversal area. We seamlessly integrate this representation into a sampling-based Model Predictive Path Integral (MPPI) controller. By fully exploiting GPU parallelism, the framework evaluates these continuous spatial constraints across thousands of simulated trajectory rollouts simultaneously in real time. Furthermore, our formulation inherently maintains spatial consistency, ensuring robust navigation even under severe visual occlusion during aggressive maneuvers. Extensive simulations and real-world experiments demonstrate that the proposed system achieves high-speed agile flight and successfully navigates unseen tracks subject to severe unmodeled gate displacements and orientation perturbations. Videos are available at https://zhaofangguo.github.io/vision_guided_mppi/

</details>


### [409] [Kinematics-Aware Latent World Models for Data-Efficient Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.07264)
*Jiazhuo Li,Linjiang Cao,Qi Liu,Xi Xiong*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes a kinematics-aware latent world model for autonomous driving that incorporates vehicle kinematic information and geometry-aware supervision into RSSM to improve sample efficiency and driving performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Data-efficient learning is challenging in autonomous driving due to high costs and safety risks of real-world interaction. Existing world-model-based RL approaches lack explicit mechanisms to encode spatial and kinematic structure essential for driving tasks.

Method: Builds upon Recurrent State-Space Model (RSSM) with vehicle kinematic information incorporated into observation encoder to ground latent transitions in physically meaningful motion dynamics. Uses geometry-aware supervision to regularize RSSM latent state to capture task-relevant spatial structure beyond pixel reconstruction.

Result: Experiments in driving simulation benchmark show consistent gains over both model-free and pixel-based world-model baselines in sample efficiency and driving performance. Ablation studies verify enhanced spatial representation quality within latent space.

Conclusion: Integrating kinematic grounding into RSSM-based world models provides a scalable and physically grounded paradigm for autonomous driving policy learning, improving long-horizon imagination fidelity and stabilizing policy optimization.

Abstract: Data-efficient learning remains a central challenge in autonomous driving due to the high cost and safety risks of large-scale real-world interaction. Although world-model-based reinforcement learning enables policy optimization through latent imagination, existing approaches often lack explicit mechanisms to encode spatial and kinematic structure essential for driving tasks. In this work, we build upon the Recurrent State-Space Model (RSSM) and propose a kinematics-aware latent world model framework for autonomous driving. Vehicle kinematic information is incorporated into the observation encoder to ground latent transitions in physically meaningful motion dynamics, while geometry-aware supervision regularizes the RSSM latent state to capture task-relevant spatial structure beyond pixel reconstruction. The resulting structured latent dynamics improve long-horizon imagination fidelity and stabilize policy optimization. Experiments in a driving simulation benchmark demonstrate consistent gains over both model-free and pixel-based world-model baselines in terms of sample efficiency and driving performance. Ablation studies further verify that the proposed design enhances spatial representation quality within the latent space. These results suggest that integrating kinematic grounding into RSSM-based world models provides a scalable and physically grounded paradigm for autonomous driving policy learning.

</details>


### [410] [Soft Rigid Hybrid Gripper with Inflatable Silicone Pockets for Tunable Frictional Grasping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.07308)
*Hoang Hiep Ly,Cong-Nhat Nguyen,Doan-Quang Tran,Quoc-Khanh Dang,Ngoc Duy Tran,Thi Thoa Mac,Anh Nguyen,Xuan-Thuan Nguyen,Tung D. Ta*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Soft-rigid hybrid gripper finger uses inflatable silicone pockets to modulate surface friction, enabling secure grasping of heavy, slippery, or fragile objects without excessive force.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional rigid grippers rely on increasing normal forces to secure objects, which can damage fragile items. There's a need for grippers that can handle diverse mechanical properties (heavy, slippery, fragile) without causing damage.

Method: Proposed a soft-rigid hybrid gripper finger combining rigid structural shells with soft, inflatable silicone pockets that can be integrated into conventional grippers. The system actively modulates surface friction by varying internal air pressure in the silicone pockets.

Result: Experimental results show that increasing internal pressure leads to proportional increase in effective coefficient of friction. The gripper can stably lift heavy/slippery objects and fragile/deformable objects (eggs, tofu, fruits, paper cups) with minimal damage by increasing friction rather than applying high force.

Conclusion: The hybrid gripper successfully addresses limitations of conventional rigid grippers by enabling secure grasping of diverse objects through friction modulation rather than force application, reducing damage to fragile items.

Abstract: Grasping objects with diverse mechanical properties, such as heavy, slippery, or fragile items, remains a significant challenge in robotics. Conventional rigid grippers typically rely on increasing the normal forces to secure an object, however, this can cause damage to fragile objects due to excessive force. To address this limitation, we propose a soft rigid hybrid gripper finger that combines rigid structural shells with soft, inflatable silicone pockets, which could be integrated into a conventional gripper. The hybrid gripper can actively modulate its surface friction by varying the internal air pressure of the silicone pockets, enabling the gripper to securely grasp objects without increasing the gripping force. This is demonstrated by fundamental experimental results, in which an increase in internal pressure leads to a proportional increase in the effective coefficient of friction. The gripping experiments also show that the integrated gripper can stably lift heavy and slippery objects or fragile, deformable objects, such as eggs, tofu, fruits, and paper cups, with minimal damage by increasing friction rather than applying high force.

</details>


### [411] [A Distributed Gaussian Process Model for Multi-Robot Mapping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.07351)
*Seth Nabarro,Mark van der Wilk,Andrew J. Davison*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: DistGP is a distributed multi-robot learning method using sparse Gaussian processes with factorized structure for collaborative global function learning via local experience and computation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable multi-robot systems to collaboratively learn global functions using only local experience and computation, without requiring centralized training or full connectivity.

Method: Uses sparse Gaussian process model with factorized structure matching multi-robot task organization, trained distributively via Gaussian belief propagation (GBP) with loopy model that supports online learning and dynamic connectivity.

Result: Outperforms Tree-Structured GPs, achieves same performance as centralized batch-trained models (though slower convergence), and surpasses DiNNO in accuracy, robustness to sparse communication, and continual learning capability.

Conclusion: DistGP provides an effective distributed learning framework for multi-robot systems that matches centralized performance while offering advantages in communication efficiency and continual learning.

Abstract: We propose DistGP: a multi-robot learning method for collaborative learning of a global function using only local experience and computation. We utilise a sparse Gaussian process (GP) model with a factorisation that mirrors the multi-robot structure of the task, and admits distributed training via Gaussian belief propagation (GBP). Our loopy model outperforms Tree-Structured GPs \cite{bui2014tree} and can be trained online and in settings with dynamic connectivity. We show that such distributed, asynchronous training can reach the same performance as a centralised, batch-trained model, albeit with slower convergence. Last, we compare to DiNNO \cite{yu2022dinno}, a distributed neural network (NN) optimiser, and find DistGP achieves superior accuracy, is more robust to sparse communication and is better able to learn continually.

</details>


### [412] [Underwater Embodied Intelligence for Autonomous Robots: A Constraint-Coupled Perspective on Planning, Control, and Deployment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.07393)
*Jingzehua Xu,Guanwen Xie,Jiwei Tang,Shuai Zhang,Xiaofan Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This review paper argues that underwater robot autonomy must address tightly coupled physical constraints (hydrodynamics, sensing, communication, energy) as integrated challenges rather than independent disturbances, proposing a constraint-coupled perspective for resilient ocean autonomy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite advances in learning-based planning and control, reliable underwater autonomy remains constrained by interacting physical limits - hydrodynamic uncertainty, partial observability, bandwidth-limited communication, and energy scarcity - that amplify each other within closed-loop systems.

Method: Develops a constraint-coupled perspective synthesizing reinforcement learning, belief-aware planning, hybrid control, multi-robot coordination, and foundation-model integration through an embodied intelligence framework. Introduces cross-layer failure taxonomy spanning epistemic, dynamic, and coordination breakdowns.

Result: Shows how different application domains (monitoring, inspection, exploration, cooperation) expose distinct stress profiles of cross-layer coupling. Analyzes how errors cascade across autonomy layers under uncertainty in real ocean environments.

Conclusion: Proposes research directions toward physics-grounded world models, certifiable learning-enabled control, communication-aware coordination, and deployment-aware system design. By internalizing constraint coupling, underwater autonomy can evolve toward resilient, scalable, and verifiable systems under real ocean conditions.

Abstract: Autonomous underwater robots are increasingly deployed for environmental monitoring, infrastructure inspection, subsea resource exploration, and long-horizon exploration. Yet, despite rapid advances in learning-based planning and control, reliable autonomy in real ocean environments remains fundamentally constrained by tightly coupled physical limits. Hydrodynamic uncertainty, partial observability, bandwidth-limited communication, and energy scarcity are not independent challenges; they interact within the closed perception-planning-control loop and often amplify one another over time. This Review develops a constraint-coupled perspective on underwater embodied intelligence, arguing that planning and control must be understood within tightly coupled sensing, communication, coordination, and resource constraints in real ocean environments. We synthesize recent progress in reinforcement learning, belief-aware planning, hybrid control, multi-robot coordination, and foundation-model integration through this embodied perspective. Across representative application domains, we show how environmental monitoring, inspection, exploration, and cooperative missions expose distinct stress profiles of cross-layer coupling.
