Recovery time reduced by 87% on Go1 quadruped, 48% on Cassie biped, 30% on H1 humanoid, and 20% on Scout wheeled platform.

Conclusion: The stability-aligned residual control approach enables rapid compensation for unobserved dynamics shifts without retraining or privileged disturbance information, preserving nominal closed-loop structure while significantly improving recovery performance across diverse robotic platforms.

Abstract: Robotic systems operating in real-world environments inevitably encounter unobserved dynamics shifts during continuous execution, including changes in actuation, mass distribution, or contact conditions. When such shifts occur mid-episode, even locally stabilizing learned policies can experience substantial transient performance degradation. While input-to-state stability guarantees bounded state deviation, it does not ensure rapid restoration of task-level performance. We address inference-time recovery under frozen policy parameters by casting adaptation as constrained disturbance shaping around a nominal stabilizing controller. We propose a stability-aligned residual control architecture in which a reinforcement learning policy trained under nominal dynamics remains fixed at deployment, and adaptation occurs exclusively through a bounded additive residual channel. A Stability Alignment Gate (SAG) regulates corrective authority through magnitude constraints, directional coherence with the nominal action, performance-conditioned activation, and adaptive gain modulation. These mechanisms preserve the nominal closed-loop structure while enabling rapid compensation for unobserved dynamics shifts without retraining or privileged disturbance information. Across mid-episode perturbations including actuator degradation, mass variation, and contact changes, the proposed method consistently reduces recovery time relative to frozen and online-adaptation baselines while maintaining near-nominal steady-state performance. Recovery time is reduced by \textbf{87\%} on the Go1 quadruped, \textbf{48\%} on the Cassie biped, \textbf{30\%} on the H1 humanoid, and \textbf{20\%} on the Scout wheeled platform on average across evaluated conditions relative to a frozen SAC policy.

</details>


### [440] [A Robust Antenna Provides Tactile Feedback in a Multi-legged Robot](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.07795)
*Zhaochen J. Xu,Juntao He,Delfin Aydan,Malaika Taylor,Tianyu Wang,Jianfeng Lin,Wesley Dyer,Daniel I. Goldman*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Multi-legged robots get tactile antennae inspired by centipedes to navigate confined spaces without vision, using antenna deformation to trigger appropriate locomotor maneuvers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-legged elongate robots struggle with robust navigation in confined spaces where body-environment contact is extensive and terrain varies rapidly, requiring better sensing capabilities for such constrained environments.

Method: Developed gradient-compliant tactile antennae (stiff base, soft tip) that map continuous antenna curvature to contact force, creating a simplified mapping from deformation to discrete collision states. Incorporated this mapping into a controller that selects locomotor maneuvers based on inferred collision states.

Result: Tactile feedback enabled reliable steering and recovery from near-stuck conditions in obstacle-rich and confined environments without requiring global environmental information or real-time vision.

Conclusion: Mechanically tuned tactile appendages can simplify sensing and enhance autonomy in elongate multi-legged robots operating in constrained spaces, demonstrating the value of bio-inspired tactile sensing for robotic navigation.

Abstract: Multi-legged elongate robots hold promise for maneuvering through complex environments. Prior work has demonstrated that reliable locomotion can be achieved using open-loop body undulation and foot placement on rugose terrain. However, robust navigation through confined spaces remains challenging when body-environment contact is extensive and terrain rheology varies rapidly. To address this challenge, we develop a pair of tactile antennae for multi-legged robots that enable real-time sensing of surrounding geometry, modeling the morphology and function of biological centipede antennae. Each antenna features gradient compliance, with a stiff base and soft tip, allowing repeated deformation and elastic recovery. Robophysical experiments reveal a relationship between continuous antenna curvature and contact force, leading to a simplified mapping from antenna deformation to inferred discrete collision states. We incorporate this mapping into a controller that selects among a set of locomotor maneuvers based on the inferred collision state. Experiments in obstacle-rich and confined environments demonstrate that tactile feedback enables reliable steering and allows the robot to recover from near-stuck conditions without requiring global environmental information or real-time vision. These results highlight how mechanically tuned tactile appendages can simplify sensing and enhance autonomy in elongate multi-legged robots operating in constrained spaces.

</details>


### [441] [Inverse Resistive Force Theory (I-RFT): Learning granular properties through robot-terrain physical interactions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.07796)
*Shipeng Liu,Feng Xue,Yifeng Zhang,Tarunika Ponnusamy,Feifei Qian*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: I-RFT: A physics-informed ML framework that integrates Granular Resistive Force Theory with Gaussian Processes to estimate granular terrain properties from contact forces during arbitrary locomotion, enabling adaptive gait optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robots need to understand granular terrain mechanical properties for safe and efficient locomotion, but existing methods rely on specific foot trajectories (vertical/horizontal) that limit applicability during natural movement.

Method: Inverse Resistive Force Theory (I-RFT) combines Granular Resistive Force Theory with Gaussian Processes to infer terrain properties from proprioceptive contact forces during arbitrary gait trajectories, preserving physical consistency while generalizing across motion primitives.

Result: I-RFT accurately estimates terrain properties across multiple gait trajectories and toe shapes, and provides quantified uncertainty over terrain resistance stress maps that could enable optimization of foot design and gait trajectories for efficient information gathering.

Conclusion: The approach establishes a foundation for data-efficient characterization of complex granular environments and opens avenues for locomotion strategies that actively adapt gait for autonomous terrain exploration.

Abstract: For robots to navigate safely and efficiently on soft, granular terrains, it is crucial to gather information about the terrain's mechanical properties, which directly affect locomotion performance. Recent research has developed robotic legs that can accurately sense ground reaction forces during locomotion. However, existing tests of granular property estimation often rely on specific foot trajectories, such as vertical penetration or horizontal shear, limiting their applicability during natural locomotion.
  To address this limitation, we introduce a physics-informed machine learning framework, Inverse Resistive Force Theory (I-RFT), which integrates the Granular Resistive Force Theory model with Gaussian Processes to infer terrain properties from proprioceptively measured contact forces under arbitrary gait trajectories. By embedding the granular force model within the learning process, I-RFT preserves physical consistency while enabling generalization across diverse motion primitives.
  Experimental results demonstrate that I-RFT accurately estimates terrain properties across multiple gait trajectories and toe shapes. Moreover, we show that the quantified uncertainty over the terrain resistance stress map could enable robots to optimize foot design and gait trajectories for efficient information gathering. This approach establishes a new foundation for data-efficient characterization of complex granular environments and opens new avenues for locomotion strategies that actively adapt gait for autonomous terrain exploration.

</details>


### [442] [Toward Global Intent Inference for Human Motion by Inverse Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.07797)
*Sarmad Mehrdad,Maxime Sabbah,Vincent Bonnet,Ludovic Righetti*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A unified time-varying cost function explains human reaching movements better than subject/posture-specific models, with joint-acceleration regulation as the dominant factor.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine if a single unified cost function can explain human reaching movements, rather than relying on subject- or posture-specific optimization criteria used in existing approaches.

Method: Used Minimal Observation Inverse Reinforcement Learning (MO-IRL) algorithm with a seven-dimensional set of candidate cost terms to efficiently estimate time-varying cost weights for planar reaching tasks. Evaluated three levels of generality: Subject-Dependent Posture-Dependent, Subject-Dependent Posture-Independent, and Subject-Independent Posture-Independent.

Result: Time-varying weights substantially improved trajectory reconstruction with 27% average RMSE reduction compared to baseline. Inferred costs consistently showed joint-acceleration regulation as dominant factor, complemented by torque-change smoothness. A single subject- and posture-agnostic time-varying cost function predicted human reaching trajectories with high accuracy.

Conclusion: A unified subject- and posture-agnostic time-varying cost function can accurately predict human reaching movements, supporting the existence of a unified optimality principle governing this class of movements.

Abstract: This paper investigates whether a single, unified cost function can explain and predict human reaching movements, in contrast with existing approaches that rely on subject- or posture-specific optimization criteria. Using the Minimal Observation Inverse Reinforcement Learning (MO-IRL) algorithm, together with a seven-dimensional set of candidate cost terms, we efficiently estimate time-varying cost weights for a standard planar reaching task. MO-IRL provides orders-of-magnitude faster convergence than bilevel formulations, while using only a fraction of the available data, enabling the practical exploration of time-varying cost structures. Three levels of generality are evaluated: Subject-Dependent Posture-Dependent, Subject-Dependent Posture-Independent, and Subject-Independent Posture-Independent. Across all cases, time-varying weights substantially improve trajectory reconstruction, yielding an average 27% reduction in RMSE compared to the baseline. The inferred costs consistently highlight a dominant role for joint-acceleration regulation, complemented by smaller contributions from torque-change smoothness. Overall, a single subject- and posture-agnostic time-varying cost function is shown to predict human reaching trajectories with high accuracy, supporting the existence of a unified optimality principle governing this class of movements.

</details>


### [443] [Preference-Conditioned Reinforcement Learning for Space-Time Efficient Online 3D Bin Packing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.07800)
*Nikita Sarawgi,Omey M. Manyar,Fan Wang,Thinh H. Nguyen,Daniel Seita,Satyandra K. Gupta*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: STEP method reduces robotic bin packing time by 44% while maintaining packing density through time-aware action selection


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current robotic bin packing systems must balance compact placement with rapid execution, where selecting alternative items or reorienting them can improve space utilization but introduces additional operational time

Method: Proposes STEP (Space-Time Efficient Packing) using a selection-based formulation that reasons over trade-off between packing benefit and operational time, with preference-conditioned Transformer-based reinforcement learning policy that evaluates multiple candidate actions

Result: Achieves 44% reduction in operational time without compromising packing density, generalizes across candidate set sizes, and integrates with standard placement modules

Conclusion: STEP enables time-aware strategies that selectively accept increased operational time when it yields meaningful spatial improvements, significantly improving efficiency in robotic bin packing

Abstract: Robotic bin packing is widely deployed in warehouse automation, with current systems achieving robust performance through heuristic and learning-based strategies. These systems must balance compact placement with rapid execution, where selecting alternative items or reorienting them can improve space utilization but introduce additional time. We propose a selection-based formulation that explicitly reasons over this trade-off: at each step, the robot evaluates multiple candidate actions, weighing expected packing benefit against estimated operational time. This enables time-aware strategies that selectively accept increased operational time when it yields meaningful spatial improvements. Our method, STEP (Space-Time Efficient Packing), uses a preference-conditioned, Transformer-based reinforcement learning policy, and allows generalization across candidate set sizes and integration with standard placement modules. It achieves a 44% reduction in operational time without compromising packing density. Additional material is available at https://step-packing.github.io.

</details>


### [444] [Uncertainty Mitigation and Intent Inference: A Dual-Mode Human-Machine Joint Planning System](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.07822)
*Zeyu Fang,Yuxin Lin,Cheng Liu,Beomyeol Yu,Zeyuan Yang,Rongqian Chen,Taeyoung Lee,Mahdi Imani,Tian Lan*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Unified human-robot joint planning system that addresses knowledge gaps and human intent uncertainty through two-way communication and probabilistic intent modeling, reducing interaction costs by 51.9% and execution time by 25.4%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing human-robot collaboration approaches treat humans as passive supervisors rather than active teammates, failing to address dual uncertainties in task knowledge and human intent that hinder effective open-world collaboration.

Method: Two-module system: 1) Uncertainty-mitigation joint planning with LLM-assisted active elicitation, hypothesis-augmented A* search, and dynamic programming for optimal querying; 2) Real-time intent-aware collaboration using probabilistic belief over human intent via spatial/directional cues for coordination-aware task selection.

Result: System validated in Gazebo simulations and real-world UAV deployments with VLM-based 3D semantic perception, achieving 51.9% reduction in interaction cost for uncertainty mitigation and 25.4% reduction in task execution time for intent-aware cooperation compared to baselines.

Conclusion: The proposed unified system enables effective human-robot collaboration by actively addressing knowledge gaps and intent uncertainty through intelligent communication and probabilistic reasoning, significantly improving efficiency in open-world environments.

Abstract: Effective human-robot collaboration in open-world environments requires joint planning under uncertain conditions. However, existing approaches often treat humans as passive supervisors, preventing autonomous agents from becoming human-like teammates that can actively model teammate behaviors, reason about knowledge gaps, query, and elicit responses through communication to resolve uncertainties. To address these limitations, we propose a unified human-robot joint planning system designed to tackle dual sources of uncertainty: task-relevant knowledge gaps and latent human intent. Our system operates in two complementary modes. First, an uncertainty-mitigation joint planning module enables two-way conversations to resolve semantic ambiguity and object uncertainty. It utilizes an LLM-assisted active elicitation mechanism and a hypothesis-augmented A^* search, subsequently computing an optimal querying policy via dynamic programming to minimize interaction and verification costs. Second, a real-time intent-aware collaboration module maintains a probabilistic belief over the human's latent task intent via spatial and directional cues, enabling dynamic, coordination-aware task selection for agents without explicit communication. We validate the proposed system in both Gazebo simulations and real-world UAV deployments integrated with a Vision-Language Model (VLM)-based 3D semantic perception pipeline. Experimental results demonstrate that the system significantly cuts the interaction cost by 51.9% in uncertainty-mitigation planning and reduces the task execution time by 25.4% in intent-aware cooperation compared to the baselines.

</details>


### [445] [Reasoning Knowledge-Gap in Drone Planning via LLM-based Active Elicitation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.07824)
*Zeyu Fang,Beomyeol Yu,Cheng Liu,Zeyuan Yang,Rongqian Chen,Yuxin Lin,Mahdi Imani,Tian Lan*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: MINT framework enables UAVs to ask minimal binary queries to resolve ambiguities instead of control handovers, improving success rates while reducing human interaction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current human-AI joint planning in UAVs relies on inefficient control handovers when facing environmental uncertainties, which is cognitively demanding for non-expert operators.

Method: Proposed Minimal Information Neuro-Symbolic Tree (MINT) framework that structures knowledge gaps into queryable format, uses LLMs to formulate optimal binary queries, integrates vision-language models for perception, voice interfaces, and UAV control modules.

Result: Significant improvement in success rate for complex search-and-rescue tasks while significantly reducing frequency of human interaction compared to exhaustive querying baselines.

Conclusion: Shifting from control takeover to active information elicitation with MINT framework enables more efficient human-AI collaboration in UAV operations with minimal human interaction.

Abstract: Human-AI joint planning in Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) typically relies on control handover when facing environmental uncertainties, which is often inefficient and cognitively demanding for non-expert operators. To address this, we propose a novel framework that shifts the collaboration paradigm from control takeover to active information elicitation. We introduce the Minimal Information Neuro-Symbolic Tree (MINT), a reasoning mechanism that explicitly structures knowledge gaps regarding obstacles and goals into a queryable format. By leveraging large language models, our system formulates optimal binary queries to resolve specific ambiguities with minimal human interaction. We demonstrate the efficacy of this approach through a comprehensive workflow integrating a vision-language model for perception, voice interfaces, and a low-level UAV control module in both high-fidelity NVIDIA Isaac simulations and real-world deployments. Experimental results show that our method achieves a significant improvement in the success rate for complex search-and-rescue tasks while significantly reducing the frequency of human interaction compared to exhaustive querying baselines.

</details>


### [446] [Physics-infused Learning for Aerial Manipulator in Winds and Near-Wall Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.07826)
*Yiming Zhang,Junyi Geng*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Combines physics-based blade-element model with learning-based residual estimator for improved aerial manipulation control under real-world aerodynamic disturbances.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current aerial manipulation systems overlook key aerodynamic effects like wind disturbances and proximity-induced flow variations. Simplified thrust models are insufficient, while high-fidelity CFD is impractical for real-time use, and learning-based models struggle with generalization.

Method: Integrates physics-based blade-element model with learning-based residual force estimator and rotor-speed allocation strategy. The blade-element model provides refined feedforward disturbance estimates, while the learning estimator predicts residual forces. Online adaptation updates both residual-force prediction and rotor-speed allocation.

Result: Demonstrates improved disturbance estimation and trajectory-tracking accuracy over conventional approaches in simulated near-wall wind environments, enabling robust wall-contact execution under challenging aerodynamic conditions.

Conclusion: The unified control framework combining physics-based modeling with learning-based estimation enables robust aerial manipulation in real-world environments with complex aerodynamic disturbances, addressing limitations of both pure physics-based and pure learning-based approaches.

Abstract: Aerial manipulation (AM) expands UAV capabilities beyond passive observation to contact-based operations at high altitudes and in otherwise inaccessible environments. Although recent advances show promise, most AM systems are developed in controlled settings that overlook key aerodynamic effects. Simplified thrust models are often insufficient to capture the nonlinear wind disturbances and proximity-induced flow variations present in real-world environments near infrastructure, while high-fidelity CFD methods remain impractical for real-time use. Learning-based models are computationally efficient at inference, but often struggle to generalize to unseen condition. This paper combines both approaches by integrating a physics-based blade-element model with a learning-based residual force estimator, along with a rotor-speed allocation strategy for disturbance compensation, resulting in a unified control framework. The blade-element model computes per-rotor aerodynamic forces under wind and provides a refined feedforward disturbance estimate. A learning-based estimator then predicts the residual forces not captured by the model, enabling compensation for unmodeled aerodynamic effects. An online adaptation mechanism further updates the residual-force prediction and rotor-speed allocation jointly to reduce the mismatch between desired and realized thrust. We evaluate this framework in both free-flight and wall-contact tracking tasks in a simulated near-wall wind environment. Results demonstrate improved disturbance estimation and trajectory-tracking accuracy over conventional approaches, enabling robust wall-contact execution under challenging aerodynamic conditions.

</details>


### [447] [Relating Reinforcement Learning to Dynamic Programming-Based Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.07844)
*Filip V. Georgiev,Kalle G. Timperi,Başak Sakçak,Steven M. LaValle*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper bridges optimal planning and reinforcement learning by developing a derandomized RL version, analyzing mathematical equivalences between cost minimization and reward maximization, and advocating for truecost optimization over arbitrary parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To bridge the gap between optimal planning and reinforcement learning, which share roots in dynamic programming but differ in approaches (deterministic vs stochastic models, cost minimization vs reward maximization, goal termination vs infinite-horizon discounting).

Method: Developed a derandomized version of RL, performed mathematical analysis of equivalences between planning and RL approaches, and conducted performance comparisons with value iteration and Dijkstra's algorithm using both deterministic and stochastic models.

Result: Established conditions for equivalence between cost minimization and reward maximization, equivalence between single-shot goal termination and infinite-horizon episodic learning, and identified conditions where discounting causes goal achievement failure.

Conclusion: Advocates for defining and optimizing truecost rather than using arbitrary parameters, and demonstrates the value of bridging planning and RL approaches through mathematical analysis and performance comparisons.

Abstract: This paper bridges some of the gap between optimal planning and reinforcement learning (RL), both of which share roots in dynamic programming applied to sequential decision making or optimal control. Whereas planning typically favors deterministic models, goal termination, and cost minimization, RL tends to favor stochastic models, infinite-horizon discounting, and reward maximization in addition to learning-related parameters such as the learning rate and greediness factor. A derandomized version of RL is developed, analyzed, and implemented to yield performance comparisons with value iteration and Dijkstra's algorithm using simple planning models. Next, mathematical analysis shows: 1) conditions under which cost minimization and reward maximization are equivalent, 2) conditions for equivalence of single-shot goal termination and infinite-horizon episodic learning, and 3) conditions under which discounting causes goal achievement to fail. The paper then advocates for defining and optimizing truecost, rather than inserting arbitrary parameters to guide operations. Performance studies are then extended to the stochastic case, using planning-oriented criteria and comparing value iteration to RL with learning rates and greediness factors.

</details>


### [448] [Viewpoint-Agnostic Grasp Pipeline using VLM and Partial Observations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.07866)
*Dilermando Almeida,Juliano Negri,Guilherme Lazzarini,Thiago H. Segreto,Ranulfo Bezerra,Ricardo V. Godoy,Marcelo Becker*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: End-to-end pipeline for language-guided grasping on mobile legged manipulators that handles occlusions and partial observations in cluttered environments, achieving 90% success rate.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robust grasping in cluttered, unstructured environments is challenging due to occlusions leading to partial observations, unreliable depth estimates, and the need for collision-free, execution-feasible approaches.

Method: End-to-end pipeline that: 1) grounds language commands in RGB using open-vocabulary detection and instance segmentation, 2) extracts object-centric point clouds from RGB-D, 3) improves geometric reliability via depth compensation and two-stage point cloud completion, 4) generates and collision-filters 6-DoF grasp candidates, and 5) selects executable grasps using safety-oriented heuristics for reachability, approach feasibility, and clearance.

Result: Achieved 90% overall success rate (9/10) on quadruped robot with arm in cluttered tabletop scenarios, compared to 30% (3/10) for view-dependent baseline, demonstrating substantially improved robustness to occlusions and partial observations.

Conclusion: The proposed end-to-end pipeline effectively bridges open-vocabulary target selection to safe grasp execution, significantly improving robustness to occlusions and partial observations in cluttered environments for mobile legged manipulators.

Abstract: Robust grasping in cluttered, unstructured environments remains challenging for mobile legged manipulators due to occlusions that lead to partial observations, unreliable depth estimates, and the need for collision-free, execution-feasible approaches. In this paper we present an end-to-end pipeline for language-guided grasping that bridges open-vocabulary target selection to safe grasp execution on a real robot. Given a natural-language command, the system grounds the target in RGB using open-vocabulary detection and promptable instance segmentation, extracts an object-centric point cloud from RGB-D, and improves geometric reliability under occlusion via back-projected depth compensation and two-stage point cloud completion. We then generate and collision-filter 6-DoF grasp candidates and select an executable grasp using safety-oriented heuristics that account for reachability, approach feasibility, and clearance. We evaluate the method on a quadruped robot with an arm in two cluttered tabletop scenarios, using paired trials against a view-dependent baseline. The proposed approach achieves a 90% overall success rate (9/10) against 30% (3/10) for the baseline, demonstrating substantially improved robustness to occlusions and partial observations in clutter.

</details>


### [449] [Choose What to Observe: Task-Aware Semantic-Geometric Representations for Visuomotor Policy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.07875)
*Haoran Ding,Liang Ma,Yaxun Yang,Wen Yang,Tianyu Liu,Anqing Duan,Xiaodan Liang,Dezhen Song,Ivan Laptev,Yoshihiko Nakamura*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A task-aware observation interface that canonicalizes visual input into semantic-geometric representations improves policy robustness to appearance shifts without policy modification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Visuomotor policies often overfit to nuisance visual factors in RGB observations, leading to brittle behavior under appearance changes like background variations and object recoloring.

Method: Given RGB image and task specification, uses SAM3 to segment relevant entities, repaints them with semantic colors on constant background (L0). For geometric tasks, injects monocular depth via Depth Anything 3 into segmented regions via depth-guided overwrite, creating unified semantic-geometric observation (L1).

Result: Evaluated on RoboMimic (Lift), ManiSkill YCB grasping, RLBench tasks under appearance shifts, and real-world Franka tasks. Preserves in-distribution performance while substantially improving robustness to OOD visual shifts across benchmarks and policy backbones.

Conclusion: The task-aware observation interface effectively canonicalizes visual input into shared representations, enhancing policy robustness to appearance variations without requiring policy modification or fine-tuning.

Abstract: Visuomotor policies learned from demonstrations often overfit to nuisance visual factors in raw RGB observations, resulting in brittle behavior under appearance shifts such as background changes and object recoloring. We propose a task-aware observation interface that canonicalizes visual input into a shared representation, improving robustness to out-of-distribution (OOD) appearance changes without modifying or fine-tuning the policy. Given an RGB image and an open-vocabulary specification of task-relevant entities, we use SAM3 to segment the target object and robot/gripper. We construct an L0 observation by repainting segmented entities with predefined semantic colors on a constant background. For tasks requiring stronger geometric cues, we further inject monocular depth from Depth Anything 3 into the segmented regions via depth-guided overwrite, yielding a unified semantic--geometric observation (L1) that remains a standard 3-channel, image-like input. We evaluate on RoboMimic (Lift), ManiSkill YCB grasping under clutter, four RLBench tasks under controlled appearance shifts, and two real-world Franka tasks (ReachX and CloseCabinet). Across benchmarks and policy backbones (Flow Matching Policy and SmolVLA), our interface preserves in-distribution performance while substantially improving robustness under OOD visual shifts.

</details>


### [450] [Identifying Influential Actions in Human-Robot Interactions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.07885)
*Haoyang Jiang,Chenfei Xu,Yuya Okadome,Yukata Nakamura*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Paper introduces transfer entropy method to identify influential robot actions in human-robot interaction, focusing on proximity effects in conversation scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To better understand and improve human-robot collaboration by identifying which specific robot actions most influence human behavior during interactions.

Method: Uses transfer entropy (TE), a statistic measuring directed information transfer between time series, to analyze complex nonlinear interactions between robot actions and human responses.

Result: The method successfully identifies key robot actions (particularly proximity-related ones) that influence human behavior during conversations with remotely controlled robot avatars.

Conclusion: Transfer entropy is an effective tool for analyzing human-robot interactions and has potential to improve robotic system design and adaptability by identifying influential actions.

Abstract: Human-robot interaction combines robotics, cognitive science, and human factors to study collaborative systems. This paper introduces a method for identifying influential robot actions using transfer entropy, a statistic that measures directed information transfer between time series. TE is effective for capturing complex, nonlinear interactions. We apply this method to analyze how robot actions affect human behavior during a conversation with a remotely controlled robot avatar. By focusing on the impact of proximity, our approach demonstrates TE's capability to identify key actions influencing human responses, highlighting its potential to improve the design and adaptability of robotic systems.

</details>


### [451] [RoboRouter: Training-Free Policy Routing for Robotic Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.07892)
*Yiteng Chen,Zhe Cao,Hongjia Ren,Chenjie Yang,Wenbo Li,Shiyi Wang,Yemin Wang,Li Zhang,Yanming Shao,Zhenjun Zhao,Huiping Zhuang,Qingyao Wu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: RoboRouter is a training-free framework that intelligently routes tasks to the best-performing policy from a heterogeneous pool, improving success rates without requiring new model training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Different robotic manipulation policy paradigms (VLA, VA, code-based) have complementary strengths but limited generalization. Instead of creating another monolithic policy, the authors propose leveraging existing approaches through intelligent routing.

Method: RoboRouter maintains a pool of heterogeneous policies, learns to select the best policy for each task through execution experience, constructs semantic task representations, retrieves similar historical tasks, predicts optimal policy choices without trial-and-error, and uses structured feedback to refine routing decisions.

Result: RoboRouter consistently outperforms individual policies, improving average success rate by more than 3% in simulation and over 13% in real-world settings while preserving execution efficiency.

Conclusion: Intelligent routing across heterogeneous, off-the-shelf policies provides a practical and scalable pathway toward building more capable robotic systems without requiring extensive training overhead.

Abstract: Research on robotic manipulation has developed a diverse set of policy paradigms, including vision-language-action (VLA) models, vision-action (VA) policies, and code-based compositional approaches. Concrete policies typically attain high success rates on specific task distributions but lim-ited generalization beyond it. Rather than proposing an other monolithic policy, we propose to leverage the complementary strengths of existing approaches through intelligent policy routing. We introduce RoboRouter, a training-free framework that maintains a pool of heterogeneous policies and learns to select the best-performing policy for each task through accumulated execution experience. Given a new task, RoboRouter constructs a semantic task representation, retrieves historical records of similar tasks, predicts the optimal policy choice without requiring trial-and-error, and incorporates structured feedback to refine subsequent routing decisions. Integrating a new policy into the system requires only lightweight evaluation and incurs no training overhead. Across simulation benchmark and real-world evaluations, RoboRouter consistently outperforms than in-dividual policies, improving average success rate by more than 3% in simulation and over 13% in real-world settings, while preserving execution efficiency. Our results demonstrate that intelligent routing across heterogeneous, off-the-shelf policies provides a practical and scalable pathway toward building more capable robotic systems.

</details>


### [452] [NaviDriveVLM: Decoupling High-Level Reasoning and Motion Planning for Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.07901)
*Ximeng Tao,Pardis Taghavi,Dimitar Filev,Reza Langari,Gaurav Pandey*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: NaviDriveVLM: A decoupled VLM framework for autonomous driving that separates reasoning (Navigator) from action generation (Driver) to preserve strong reasoning while enabling efficient training for precise control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VLM-based autonomous driving systems face a trade-off: large models offer good reasoning but are costly to adapt for precise control, while small models are efficient to train but have weaker reasoning capabilities.

Method: Proposes NaviDriveVLM with a decoupled architecture: a large-scale Navigator for semantic understanding and reasoning, and a lightweight trainable Driver for action generation. This provides explicit interpretable intermediate representations for downstream planning.

Result: Experiments on nuScenes benchmark show NaviDriveVLM outperforms large VLM baselines in end-to-end motion planning.

Conclusion: The decoupled framework successfully addresses the trade-off between reasoning and control in VLM-based autonomous driving, preserving reasoning ability while reducing training costs and providing interpretable intermediate representations.

Abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) have emerged as a promising direction for end-to-end autonomous driving (AD) by jointly modeling visual observations, driving context, and language-based reasoning. However, existing VLM-based systems face a trade-off between high-level reasoning and motion planning: large models offer strong semantic understanding but are costly to adapt for precise control, whereas small VLM models can be fine-tuned efficiently but often exhibit weaker reasoning. We propose NaviDriveVLM, a decoupled framework that separates reasoning from action generation using a large-scale Navigator and a lightweight trainable Driver. This design preserves reasoning ability, reduces training cost, and provides an explicit interpretable intermediate representation for downstream planning. Experiments on the nuScenes benchmark show that NaviDriveVLM outperforms large VLM baselines in end-to-end motion planning.

</details>


### [453] [Long-Short Term Agents for Pure-Vision Bronchoscopy Robotic Autonomy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.07909)
*Junyang Wu,Mingyi Luo,Fangfang Xie,Minghui Zhang,Hanxiao Zhang,Chunxi Zhang,Junhao Wang,Jiayuan Sun,Yun Gu,Guang-Zhong Yang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Vision-only autonomous bronchoscopic navigation framework using hierarchical agents and world-model critic achieves preclinical feasibility without external tracking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current robot-assisted endoluminal navigation relies on external tracking technologies (electromagnetic tracking, shape sensing) which increase hardware complexity and are vulnerable to anatomical mismatch. Limited endoscopic field of view and dynamic artifacts make accurate intraoperative navigation challenging.

Method: Vision-only autonomy framework using hierarchical long-short agents: short-term reactive agent for continuous low-latency motion control, and long-term strategic agent for decision support at ambiguous points. When agents conflict, a world-model critic predicts future visual states for candidate actions and selects the action whose predicted state best matches the target view. Uses preoperative CT-derived virtual targets and live endoscopic video without external tracking.

Result: System reached all planned segmental targets in high-fidelity airway phantom, maintained 80% success to eighth generation ex vivo, and achieved in vivo navigation performance comparable to expert bronchoscopist.

Conclusion: Results support preclinical feasibility of sensor-free autonomous bronchoscopic navigation using vision-only framework with hierarchical agents and world-model critic.

Abstract: Accurate intraoperative navigation is essential for robot-assisted endoluminal intervention, but remains difficult because of limited endoscopic field of view and dynamic artifacts. Existing navigation platforms often rely on external localization technologies, such as electromagnetic tracking or shape sensing, which increase hardware complexity and remain vulnerable to intraoperative anatomical mismatch. We present a vision-only autonomy framework that performs long-horizon bronchoscopic navigation using preoperative CT-derived virtual targets and live endoscopic video, without external tracking during navigation. The framework uses hierarchical long-short agents: a short-term reactive agent for continuous low-latency motion control, and a long-term strategic agent for decision support at anatomically ambiguous points. When their recommendations conflict, a world-model critic predicts future visual states for candidate actions and selects the action whose predicted state best matches the target view. We evaluated the system in a high-fidelity airway phantom, three ex vivo porcine lungs, and a live porcine model. The system reached all planned segmental targets in the phantom, maintained 80\% success to the eighth generation ex vivo, and achieved in vivo navigation performance comparable to the expert bronchoscopist. These results support the preclinical feasibility of sensor-free autonomous bronchoscopic navigation.

</details>


### [454] [Omnidirectional Humanoid Locomotion on Stairs via Unsafe Stepping Penalty and Sparse LiDAR Elevation Mapping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.07928)
*Yuzhi Jiang,Yujun Liang,Junhao Li,Han Ding,Lijun Zhu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A novel framework for humanoid robot stair traversal using dense unsafe stepping penalties and enhanced elevation mapping with Edge-Guided Asymmetric U-Net for reliable foothold selection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Humanoid robots are inherently unstable on stairs due to high center of gravity. Existing methods have blind zones from forward-facing cameras and sparse post-contact penalties that lead to inefficient learning and suboptimal strategies for omnidirectional stair traversal.

Method: Single-stage training framework with dense unsafe stepping penalties providing continuous feedback. Rolling point-cloud mapping system with spatiotemporal confidence decay and self-protection zone for stable elevation maps. Edge-Guided Asymmetric U-Net (EGAU) refines maps to mitigate reconstruction distortion from sparse LiDAR on stair risers.

Result: Achieves near-100% safe stepping rate on stair terrains in simulation, maintains high safe stepping rate in real-world deployments, and completes continuous long-distance walking tests on complex outdoor terrains.

Conclusion: The proposed method enables safe omnidirectional stair traversal for humanoid robots with reliable sim-to-real transfer and long-term stability through improved terrain perception and continuous foothold safety feedback.

Abstract: Humanoid robots, characterized by numerous degrees of freedom and a high center of gravity, are inherently unstable. Safe omnidirectional locomotion on stairs requires both omnidirectional terrain perception and reliable foothold selection. Existing methods often rely on forward-facing depth cameras, which create blind zones that restrict omnidirectional mobility. Furthermore, sparse post-contact unsafe stepping penalties lead to low learning efficiency and suboptimal strategies. To realize safe stair-traversal gaits, this paper introduces a single-stage training framework incorporating a dense unsafe stepping penalty that provides continuous feedback as the foot approaches a hazardous placement. To obtain stable and reliable elevation maps, we build a rolling point-cloud mapping system with spatiotemporal confidence decay and a self-protection zone mechanism, producing temporally consistent local maps. These maps are further refined by an Edge-Guided Asymmetric U-Net (EGAU), which mitigates reconstruction distortion caused by sparse LiDAR returns on stair risers. Simulation and real-robot experiments show that the proposed method achieves a near-100\% safe stepping rate on stair terrains in simulation, while maintaining a remarkably high safe stepping rate in real-world deployments. Furthermore, it completes a continuous long-distance walking test on complex outdoor terrains, demonstrating reliable sim-to-real transfer and long-term stability.

</details>


### [455] [Unified Structural-Hydrodynamic Modeling of Underwater Underactuated Mechanisms and Soft Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.07939)
*Chenrui Zhang,Yiyuan Zhang,Yunfei Ye,Junkai Chen,Haozhe Wang,Cecilia Laschi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A trajectory-driven global optimization framework for unified structural-hydrodynamic modeling of underwater multibody systems, enabling accurate parameter identification for both underactuated mechanisms and soft robotic systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Underwater robots need accurate modeling, but underactuated and soft robotic systems are challenging to model due to high-dimensional structural and hydrodynamic parameters. Current approaches struggle with coupled internal and external parameter identification.

Method: Proposes a trajectory-driven global optimization framework inspired by CMA-ES that simultaneously identifies coupled internal elastic, damping, and distributed hydrodynamic parameters through trajectory-level matching between simulation and experimental motion.

Result: Validated on underactuated multibody mechanism with <5% normalized end effector position error across various conditions. Successfully transferred to soft octopus arm and assembled into swimming octopus robot with realistic whole-body behavior without additional calibration.

Conclusion: The framework demonstrates scalability and transferability across underwater underactuated and soft robotic systems, enabling high-fidelity modeling without manual retuning between different robotic platforms.

Abstract: Underwater robots are widely deployed for ocean exploration and manipulation. Underactuated mechanisms are particularly advantageous in aquatic environments, as reducing actuator count lowers the risk of motor leakage while introducing inherent mechanical compliance. However, accurate modeling of underwater underactuated and soft robotic systems remains challenging because it requires identifying a high-dimensional set of internal structural and external hydrodynamic parameters. In this work, we propose a trajectory-driven global optimization framework for unified structural-hydrodynamic modeling of underwater multibody systems. Inspired by the Covariance Matrix Adaptation Evolution Strategy (CMA-ES), the proposed approach simultaneously identifies coupled internal elastic, damping, and distributed hydrodynamic parameters through trajectory-level matching between simulation and experimental motion. This enables high-fidelity reproduction of both underactuated mechanisms and compliant soft robotic systems in underwater environments. We first validate the framework on a link-by-link underactuated multibody mechanism, demonstrating accurate identification of distributed hydrodynamic coefficients, with a normalized end effector position error below 5% across multiple trajectories, varying initial conditions, and both active-passive and fully passive configurations. The identified modeling strategy is then transferred to a single octopus-inspired soft arm, showing strong real-to-sim consistency without manual retuning. Finally, eight identified arms are assembled into a swimming octopus robot, where the unified parameter set enables realistic whole body behavior without additional parameter calibration. These results demonstrate the scalability and transferability of the proposed structural-hydrodynamic modeling framework across underwater underactuated and soft robotic systems.

</details>


### [456] [VORL-EXPLORE: A Hybrid Learning Planning Approach to Multi-Robot Exploration in Dynamic Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.07973)
*Ning Liu,Sen Shen,Zheng Li,Sheng Liu,Dongkun Han,Shangke Lyu,Thomas Braunl*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: VORL-EXPLORE: A hybrid learning-planning framework for multi-robot exploration that couples task allocation with motion execution through execution fidelity signals, reducing bottlenecks and improving efficiency in dense dynamic environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional hierarchical multi-robot exploration decouples frontier allocation from local navigation, causing brittleness in dense dynamic environments. This leads to robots clustering at bottlenecks, oscillatory replanning, and redundant coverage due to allocators lacking awareness of execution difficulty.

Method: Proposes execution fidelity - a shared estimate of local navigability that couples task allocation with motion execution. Uses fidelity-coupled Voronoi objective with inter-robot repulsion to reduce contention. Implements risk-aware adaptive arbitration between global A* guidance and reactive RL policy. Features online self-supervised recalibration of fidelity model using pseudo-labels from recent progress and safety outcomes.

Result: Extensive experiments in randomized grids and Gazebo factory scenarios show high success rates, shorter path length, lower overlap, and robust collision avoidance. Dedicated severe-traffic ablation demonstrates adaptation to non-stationary obstacles without manual risk tuning.

Conclusion: VORL-EXPLORE effectively addresses limitations of decoupled hierarchical exploration by integrating execution fidelity, enabling more efficient and robust multi-robot exploration in dense dynamic environments through hybrid learning-planning approach with online adaptation.

Abstract: Hierarchical multi-robot exploration commonly decouples frontier allocation from local navigation, which can make the system brittle in dense and dynamic environments. Because the allocator lacks direct awareness of execution difficulty, robots may cluster at bottlenecks, trigger oscillatory replanning, and generate redundant coverage. We propose VORL-EXPLORE, a hybrid learning and planning framework that addresses this limitation through execution fidelity, a shared estimate of local navigability that couples task allocation with motion execution. This fidelity signal is incorporated into a fidelity-coupled Voronoi objective with inter-robot repulsion to reduce contention before it emerges. It also drives a risk-aware adaptive arbitration mechanism between global A* guidance and a reactive reinforcement learning policy, balancing long-range efficiency with safe interaction in confined spaces. The framework further supports online self-supervised recalibration of the fidelity model using pseudo-labels derived from recent progress and safety outcomes, enabling adaptation to non-stationary obstacles without manual risk tuning. We evaluate this capability separately in a dedicated severe-traffic ablation. Extensive experiments in randomized grids and a Gazebo factory scenario show high success rates, shorter path length, lower overlap, and robust collision avoidance. The source code will be made publicly available upon acceptance.

</details>


### [457] [Aero-Promptness: Drag-Aware Aerodynamic Manipulability for Propeller-driven Vehicles](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.07998)
*Antonio Franchi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: DAAM is a geometric control allocation framework for redundant multirotors that uses Riemannian metrics to account for motor torque limits and aerodynamic drag, providing smooth optimal allocations with natural saturation avoidance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing control allocation methods for redundant multirotors don't adequately handle motor torque limits and aerodynamic drag effects, leading to potential saturation and inefficient thrust generation.

Method: Introduces Drag-Aware Aerodynamic Manipulability (DAAM) by equipping propeller spin-rate space with Riemannian metrics based on remaining symmetric acceleration capacity, mapping to generalized force space to create state-dependent manipulability volumes, and using log-determinant as barrier function.

Result: The framework provides optimal allocations that form smooth embedded manifolds locally, with geometric characterization of global jump discontinuities from actuator limits and spin-rate sign transitions, while being invariant to coordinate scaling.

Conclusion: DAAM offers a principled geometric approach for control allocation in redundant multirotors that explicitly handles drag effects and motor limits, preventing saturation while maintaining smooth control performance.

Abstract: This work introduces the Drag-Aware Aerodynamic Manipulability (DAAM), a geometric framework for control allocation in redundant multirotors. By equipping the propeller spin-rate space with a Riemannian metric based on the remaining symmetric acceleration capacity of each motor, the formulation explicitly accounts for motor torque limits and aerodynamic drag. Mapping this metric through the nonlinear thrust law to the generalized force space yields a state-dependent manipulability volume. The log-determinant of this volume acts as a natural barrier function, strictly penalizing drag-induced saturation and low-spin thrust loss. Optimizing this volume along the allocation fibers provides a redundancy resolution strategy inherently invariant to arbitrary coordinate scaling in the generalized-force space. Analytically, we prove that the resulting optimal allocations locally form smooth embedded manifolds, and we geometrically characterize the global jump discontinuities that inevitably arise from physical actuator limits and spin-rate sign transitions.

</details>


### [458] [Dual-Horizon Hybrid Internal Model for Low-Gravity Quadrupedal Jumping with Hardware-in-the-Loop Validation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.07999)
*Haozhe Xu,Yifei Zhao,Wenhao Feng,Zhipeng Wang,Hongrui Sang,Cheng Cheng,Xiuxian Li,Zhen Yin,Bin He*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A dual-horizon hybrid internal model enables continuous quadrupedal jumping in lunar gravity using only proprioceptive sensing, validated on a mixed-reality testbed that emulates lunar terrain and reduced gravity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Continuous pronking in lunar gravity is challenging due to prolonged flight phases and sparse ground contact, which increases landing impact sensitivity and makes stable attitude regulation over rough terrain difficult. Existing approaches only address single jumps on flat surfaces and lack continuous-terrain solutions and realistic hardware validation.

Method: Developed a Dual-Horizon Hybrid Internal Model with two temporal encoders: short-horizon branch models rapid vertical dynamics with explicit vertical velocity estimation, while long-horizon branch models horizontal motion trends and center-of-mass height evolution across jump cycles. Also created MATRIX platform - a digital-twin-driven mixed-reality testbed that offloads gravity via pulley-counterweight mechanism and maps Unreal Engine lunar terrain to motion platform and treadmill in real time.

Result: Demonstrated continuous jumping of a quadruped robot under lunar-gravity emulation across cratered lunar-like terrain using the MATRIX platform, showing stable and continuous jumping under extended aerial phases characteristic of lunar gravity.

Conclusion: The proposed dual-horizon model enables stable continuous quadrupedal jumping in lunar gravity using only proprioceptive sensing, and the MATRIX platform provides realistic hardware-in-the-loop validation for planetary exploration locomotion research.

Abstract: Locomotion under reduced gravity is commonly realized through jumping, yet continuous pronking in lunar gravity remains challenging due to prolonged flight phases and sparse ground contact. The extended aerial duration increases landing impact sensitivity and makes stable attitude regulation over rough planetary terrain difficult. Existing approaches primarily address single jumps on flat surfaces and lack both continuous-terrain solutions and realistic hardware validation. This work presents a Dual-Horizon Hybrid Internal Model for continuous quadrupedal jumping under lunar gravity using proprioceptive sensing only. Two temporal encoders capture complementary time scales: a short-horizon branch models rapid vertical dynamics with explicit vertical velocity estimation, while a long-horizon branch models horizontal motion trends and center-of-mass height evolution across the jump cycle. The fused representation enables stable and continuous jumping under extended aerial phases characteristic of lunar gravity. To provide hardware-in-the-loop validation, we develop the MATRIX (Mixed-reality Adaptive Testbed for Robotic Integrated eXploration) platform, a digital-twin-driven system that offloads gravity through a pulley-counterweight mechanism and maps Unreal Engine lunar terrain to a motion platform and treadmill in real time. Using MATRIX, we demonstrate continuous jumping of a quadruped robot under lunar-gravity emulation across cratered lunar-like terrain.

</details>


### [459] [Vector Field Augmented Differentiable Policy Learning for Vision-Based Drone Racing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.08019)
*Yang Su,Feng Yu,Yu Hu,Xinze Niu,Linzuo Zhang,Fangyu Sun,Danping Zou*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: DiffRacing: A differentiable policy learning framework for drone racing that uses vector fields to provide continuous gradient signals for gate traversal, with a Delta Action Model for sim-to-real transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autonomous drone racing requires agile, high-speed flight with reliable obstacle avoidance, but differentiable-physics-based methods struggle because gate traversal objectives are hard to express as smooth, differentiable losses.

Method: DiffRacing integrates differentiable losses and vector fields into training to provide continuous gradient signals, balancing obstacle avoidance and gate traversal. Includes a differentiable Delta Action Model to compensate for dynamics mismatch for sim-to-real transfer.

Result: Achieves superior sample efficiency, faster convergence, and robust flight performance in both simulation and real-world experiments.

Conclusion: Vector fields can effectively augment traditional gradient-based policy learning with task-specific geometric priors for drone racing applications.

Abstract: Autonomous drone racing in complex environments requires agile, high-speed flight while maintaining reliable obstacle avoidance. Differentiable-physics-based policy learning has recently demonstrated high sample efficiency and remarkable performance across various tasks, including agile drone flight and quadruped locomotion. However, applying such methods to drone racing remains difficult, as key objective like gate traversal are inherently hard to express as smooth, differentiable losses. To address these challenges, we propose DiffRacing, a novel vector field-augmented differentiable policy learning framework. DiffRacing integrates differentiable losses and vector fields into the training process to provide continuous and stable gradient signals, balancing obstacle avoidance and high-speed gate traversal. In addition, a differentiable Delta Action Model compensates for dynamics mismatch, enabling efficient sim-to-real transfer without explicit system identification. Extensive simulation and real-world experiments demonstrate that DiffRacing achieves superior sample efficiency, faster convergence, and robust flight performance, thereby demonstrating that vector fields can augment traditional gradient-based policy learning with a task-specific geometric prior.

</details>


### [460] [AffordGrasp: Cross-Modal Diffusion for Affordance-Aware Grasp Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.08021)
*Xiaofei Wu,Yi Zhang,Yumeng Liu,Yuexin Ma,Yujiao Shi,Xuming He*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: AffordGrasp: A diffusion-based framework for generating physically stable and semantically faithful human grasping poses by bridging the modality gap between 3D objects and textual instructions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing semantic grasping approaches struggle with the large modality gap between 3D object representations and textual instructions, often lacking explicit spatial or semantic constraints, leading to physically invalid or semantically inconsistent grasps in AR/VR and embodied AI applications.

Method: 1) Introduces scalable annotation pipeline for fine-grained structured language labels; 2) Uses affordance-aware latent representation of hand poses with dual-conditioning diffusion process; 3) Incorporates distribution adjustment module for physical contact consistency and semantic alignment.

Result: Substantial improvements over state-of-the-art methods across four instruction-augmented benchmarks (HO-3D, OakInk, GRAB, AffordPose) in grasp quality, semantic accuracy, and diversity.

Conclusion: AffordGrasp successfully bridges the modality gap between 3D objects and textual instructions, producing physically stable and semantically faithful human grasps with high precision for natural hand-object interactions.

Abstract: Generating human grasping poses that accurately reflect both object geometry and user-specified interaction semantics is essential for natural hand-object interactions in AR/VR and embodied AI. However, existing semantic grasping approaches struggle with the large modality gap between 3D object representations and textual instructions, and often lack explicit spatial or semantic constraints, leading to physically invalid or semantically inconsistent grasps. In this work, we present AffordGrasp, a diffusion-based framework that produces physically stable and semantically faithful human grasps with high precision. We first introduce a scalable annotation pipeline that automatically enriches hand-object interaction datasets with fine-grained structured language labels capturing interaction intent. Building upon these annotations, AffordGrasp integrates an affordance-aware latent representation of hand poses with a dual-conditioning diffusion process, enabling the model to jointly reason over object geometry, spatial affordances, and instruction semantics. A distribution adjustment module further enforces physical contact consistency and semantic alignment. We evaluate AffordGrasp across four instruction-augmented benchmarks derived from HO-3D, OakInk, GRAB, and AffordPose, and observe substantial improvements over state-of-the-art methods in grasp quality, semantic accuracy, and diversity.

</details>


### [461] [See and Switch: Vision-Based Branching for Interactive Robot-Skill Programming](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.08057)
*Petr Vanc,Jan Kristof Behrens,Václav Hlaváč,Karla Stepanova*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: See & Switch: A vision-based interactive teaching framework for programming robots by demonstration using conditional task graphs with reliable branch selection via eye-in-hand images.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Programming robots by demonstration (PbD) is intuitive but struggles with real-world variability. While conditional task graphs are expressive and incremental, they require reliable perception-grounded online branch selection, which current approaches lack.

Method: See & Switch uses user-extendable graphs of skill parts connected via decision states for conditional branching. It employs a vision-based Switcher with eye-in-hand images (high-dimensional) for branch selection and anomaly detection, integrated with kinesthetic teaching, joystick control, and hand gestures via an input-modality-abstraction layer.

Result: The system achieves 90.7% accuracy for branch selection and 87.9% accuracy for anomaly detection across 576 real-robot rollouts. Validated on three challenging dexterous manipulation tasks with 8 participants in user studies.

Conclusion: See & Switch provides a teaching modality-independent framework that enables efficient in-situ recovery demonstrations and reliably performs branch selection and anomaly detection for novice users using vision-based conditional task graphs.

Abstract: Programming robots by demonstration (PbD) is an intuitive concept, but scaling it to real-world variability remains a challenge for most current teaching frameworks. Conditional task graphs are very expressive and can be defined incrementally, which fits very well with the PbD idea. However, acting using conditional task graphs requires reliable perception-grounded online branch selection. In this paper, we present See & Switch, an interactive teaching-and-execution framework that represents tasks as user-extendable graphs of skill parts connected via decision states (DS), enabling conditional branching during replay. Unlike prior approaches that rely on manual branching or low-dimensional signals (e.g., proprioception), our vision-based Switcher uses eye-in-hand images (high-dimensional) to select among competing successor skill parts and to detect out-of-distribution contexts that require new demonstrations. We integrate kinesthetic teaching, joystick control, and hand gestures via an input-modality-abstraction layer and demonstrate that our proposed method is teaching modality-independent, enabling efficient in-situ recovery demonstrations. The system is validated in experiments on three challenging dexterous manipulation tasks. We evaluate our method under diverse conditions and furthermore conduct user studies with 8 participants. We show that the proposed method reliably performs branch selection and anomaly detection for novice users, achieving 90.7 % and 87.9 % accuracy, respectively, across 576 real-robot rollouts. We provide all code and data required to reproduce our experiments at http://imitrob.ciirc.cvut.cz/publications/seeandswitch.

</details>


### [462] [Adaptive Vision-Based Control of Redundant Robots with Null-Space Interaction for Human-Robot Collaboration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.08089)
*Xiangjie Yan,Chen Chen,Xiang Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A new control scheme for redundant robots enables safer human-robot collaboration by decoupling task space and null space control, allowing autonomous task execution while maintaining human interaction capabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Human-robot collaboration has transformative potential across various domains (assistive technology, manufacturing, surgery), but achieving collaboration that outperforms individual human or robot performance remains challenging. Current systems need better safety and effectiveness in dynamic, unknown environments.

Method: Proposed a dual-control scheme: 1) adaptive vision-based control in task space for autonomous operation without prior calibration, and 2) interactive control in null space for human collaboration. This decoupling allows the robot to perform main tasks while safely interacting with humans to handle unforeseen changes like collisions or temporary needs.

Result: The control scheme's stability was rigorously proved using Lyapunov methods, guaranteeing convergence of position error in task space and damping model in null space. Experimental validation with an AR-guided robot manipulator demonstrated effective performance.

Conclusion: The proposed control scheme enables safer and more effective human-robot collaboration by leveraging redundant robot configurations, allowing robots to autonomously perform tasks while maintaining interactive capabilities for dynamic human collaboration needs.

Abstract: Human-robot collaboration aims to extend human ability through cooperation with robots. This technology is currently helping people with physical disabilities, has transformed the manufacturing process of companies, improved surgical performance, and will likely revolutionize the daily lives of everyone in the future. Being able to enhance the performance of both sides, such that human-robot collaboration outperforms a single robot/human, remains an open issue. For safer and more effective collaboration, a new control scheme has been proposed for redundant robots in this paper, consisting of an adaptive vision-based control term in task space and an interactive control term in null space. Such a formulation allows the robot to autonomously carry out tasks in an unknown environment without prior calibration while also interacting with humans to deal with unforeseen changes (e.g., potential collision, temporary needs) under the redundant configuration. The decoupling between task space and null space helps to explore the collaboration safely and effectively without affecting the main task of the robot end-effector. The stability of the closed-loop system has been rigorously proved with Lyapunov methods, and both the convergence of the position error in task space and that of the damping model in null space are guaranteed. The experimental results of a robot manipulator guided with the technology of augmented reality (AR) are presented to illustrate the performance of the control scheme.

</details>


### [463] [DeReCo: Decoupling Representation and Coordination Learning for Object-Adaptive Decentralized Multi-Robot Cooperative Transport](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.08111)
*Kazuki Shibata,Ryosuke Sota,Shandil Dhiresh Bosch,Yuki Kadokawa,Tsurumine Yoshihisa,Takamitsu Matsubara*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: DeReCo decouples representation and coordination learning in multi-robot cooperative transport to improve sample efficiency and generalization across diverse objects.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current decentralized multi-robot transport methods suffer from bidirectional interference between object representation learning (under partial observability) and coordination learning (under MARL non-stationarity), leading to sample-inefficient training and poor generalization.

Method: Three-stage training: (1) centralized coordination learning with privileged object info, (2) reconstruction of object-dependent representations from local observations, and (3) progressive removal of privileged info for decentralized execution.

Result: Outperforms baselines on 3 training objects, generalizes to 6 unseen objects with varying masses/friction in simulation, and achieves superior performance on 2 unseen objects in real-robot experiments.

Conclusion: Decoupling representation and coordination learning mitigates interference, enables stable sample-efficient training, and improves generalization in object-adaptive multi-robot cooperative transport.

Abstract: Generalizing decentralized multi-robot cooperative transport across objects with diverse shapes and physical properties remains a fundamental challenge. Under decentralized execution, two key challenges arise: object-dependent representation learning under partial observability and coordination learning in multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) under non-stationarity. A typical approach jointly optimizes object-dependent representations and coordinated policies in an end-to-end manner while randomizing object shapes and physical properties during training. However, this joint optimization tightly couples representation and coordination learning, introducing bidirectional interference: inaccurate representations under partial observability destabilize coordination learning, while non-stationarity in MARL further degrades representation learning, resulting in sample-inefficient training. To address this structural coupling, we propose DeReCo, a novel MARL framework that decouples representation and coordination learning for object-adaptive multi-robot cooperative transport, improving sample efficiency and generalization across objects and transport scenarios. DeReCo adopts a three-stage training strategy: (1) centralized coordination learning with privileged object information, (2) reconstruction of object-dependent representations from local observations, and (3) progressive removal of privileged information for decentralized execution. This decoupling mitigates interference between representation and coordination learning and enables stable and sample-efficient training. Experimental results show that DeReCo outperforms baselines in simulation on three training objects, generalizes to six unseen objects with varying masses and friction coefficients, and achieves superior performance on two unseen objects in real-robot experiments.

</details>


### [464] [Towards Human-Like Manipulation through RL-Augmented Teleoperation and Mixture-of-Dexterous-Experts VLA](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.08122)
*Tutian Tang,Xingyu Ji,Wanli Xing,Ce Hao,Wenqiang Xu,Lin Shao,Cewu Lu,Qiaojun Yu,Jiangmiao Pang,Kaifeng Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes IMCopilot for RL-based atomic skills and MoDE-VLA architecture to integrate force/tactile sensing into VLAs for dexterous bimanual manipulation, achieving 2x success rate improvement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VLA models are limited to simple pick-and-place tasks with low-DOF end-effectors, but extending to human-like bimanual dexterous manipulation (especially contact-rich in-hand operations) faces challenges in data acquisition, multi-skill learning, and multimodal sensory fusion.

Method: Two-component framework: 1) IMCopilot - RL-trained atomic skills for teleoperation assistance and low-level execution primitives; 2) MoDE-VLA - Mixture-of-Dexterous-Experts architecture that integrates force/tactile modalities into pretrained VLA backbone using residual injection mechanism for contact-aware refinement.

Result: Validated on four tasks of escalating complexity, demonstrating doubled success rate improvement over baseline in dexterous contact-rich tasks.

Conclusion: The integrated framework successfully addresses key bottlenecks in extending VLA models to complex bimanual dexterous manipulation by combining RL-based atomic skills with multimodal sensory fusion while preserving pretrained knowledge.

Abstract: While Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have demonstrated remarkable success in robotic manipulation, their application has largely been confined to low-degree-of-freedom end-effectors performing simple, vision-guided pick-and-place tasks. Extending these models to human-like, bimanual dexterous manipulation-specifically contact-rich in-hand operations-introduces critical challenges in high-fidelity data acquisition, multi-skill learning, and multimodal sensory fusion. In this paper, we propose an integrated framework to address these bottlenecks, built upon two components. First, we introduce IMCopilot (In-hand Manipulation Copilot), a suite of reinforcement learning-trained atomic skills that plays a dual role: it acts as a shared-autonomy assistant to simplify teleoperation data collection, and it serves as a callable low-level execution primitive for the VLA. Second, we present MoDE-VLA (Mixture-of-Dexterous-Experts VLA), an architecture that seamlessly integrates heterogeneous force and tactile modalities into a pretrained VLA backbone. By utilizing a residual injection mechanism, MoDE-VLA enables contact-aware refinement without degrading the model's pretrained knowledge. We validate our approach on four tasks of escalating complexity, demonstrating doubled success rate improvement over the baseline in dexterous contact-rich tasks.

</details>


### [465] [SaiVLA-0: Cerebrum--Pons--Cerebellum Tripartite Architecture for Compute-Aware Vision-Language-Action](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.08124)
*Xiang Shi,Wenlong Huang,Menglin Zou,Xinhai Sun*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A neuroscience-inspired modular VLA architecture with frozen Cerebrum for priors, Pons Adapter for integration, and Cerebellum for fast control, featuring movement-stabilized wrist views and efficient training protocols.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To create a more biologically plausible and efficient Vision-Language-Action system by drawing inspiration from neuroscience, specifically the functional separation of brain regions (cerebrum, pons, cerebellum) to achieve modularity, stability, and computational efficiency.

Method: Proposes a triad architecture: 1) Frozen Cerebrum provides stable multimodal priors, 2) Pons Adapter integrates cortical features with proprioception and compiles intent, 3) Cerebellum (ParaCAT) performs fast parallel categorical decoding for online control with stability mechanisms. Uses movement-stabilized wrist ROIs tied to end-effector via calibrated projection, fixed-ratio schedule, and two-stage feature caching.

Result: Preliminary evidence shows split feature caching reduces training time from 7.5h to 4.5h and improves average success from 86.5% to 92.5% on LIBERO benchmark. SaiVLA0 reaches 99.0% mean success. The modular design allows independent upgrades: Cerebrum upgrades only retrain Pons, robot changes only train Cerebellum, and cerebellum-only RL can refine control.

Conclusion: The neuroscience-inspired modular architecture offers computational efficiency, reproducibility, and performance improvements while maintaining biological plausibility. The concept-and-protocol paper outlines a timing protocol for verifying efficiency gains and demonstrates promising preliminary results on robotic manipulation tasks.

Abstract: We revisit Vision-Language-Action through a neuroscience-inspired triad. Biologically, the Cerebrum provides stable high-level multimodal priors and remains frozen; the Pons Adapter integrates these cortical features with real-time proprioceptive inputs and compiles intent into execution-ready tokens; and the Cerebellum (ParaCAT) performs fast, parallel categorical decoding for online control, with hysteresis/EMA/temperature/entropy for stability. A fixed-ratio schedule and two-stage feature caching make the system compute-aware and reproducible. Inspired by active, foveated vision, our wrist ROIs are geometrically tied to the end-effector via calibrated projection, providing a movement-stabilized, high-resolution view that is sensitive to fine-grained pose changes and complements the global context of the main view.
  The design is modular: upgrading the Cerebrum only retrains the Pons; changing robots only trains the Cerebellum; cerebellum-only RL can further refine control without touching high-level semantics. As a concept-and-protocol paper with preliminary evidence, we outline a timing protocol under matched conditions (GPU, resolution, batch) to verify anticipated efficiency gains. We also report preliminary LIBERO evidence showing that split feature caching reduces training time (7.5h to 4.5h) and improves average success (86.5% to 92.5%) under official N1.5 head-only training, and that SaiVLA0 reaches 99.0% mean success.

</details>


### [466] [TRIAGE: Type-Routed Interventions via Aleatoric-Epistemic Gated Estimation in Robotic Manipulation and Adaptive Perception -- Don't Treat All Uncertainty the Same](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.08128)
