*Divake Kumar,Sina Tayebati,Devashri Naik,Patrick Poggi,Amanda Sofie Rios,Nilesh Ahuja,Amit Ranjan Trivedi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Lightweight framework decomposes uncertainty into aleatoric (observation corruption) and epistemic (model mismatch) components to trigger type-specific corrective responses in robotic systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current uncertainty-aware systems collapse uncertainty into a single scalar, obscuring whether uncertainty comes from corrupted observations or model mismatch, leading to inappropriate corrective actions that degrade performance.

Method: Post hoc framework estimates aleatoric uncertainty via Mahalanobis density model on observation deviations, and epistemic uncertainty via noise-robust forward dynamics ensemble. These orthogonal signals trigger specific responses: aleatoric triggers observation recovery, epistemic moderates control actions, and both guide adaptive perception model selection.

Result: In robotic manipulation, decomposed controller improves task success from 59.4% to 80.4% under compound perturbations, outperforming combined uncertainty baseline by up to 21.0%. In adaptive tracking on MOT17, uncertainty-guided model selection reduces compute by 58.2% while preserving detection quality within 0.4%.

Conclusion: Decomposing uncertainty into aleatoric and epistemic components enables type-specific responses that significantly improve both control and perception performance in robotic systems, with demonstrated benefits in manipulation tasks and adaptive tracking.

Abstract: Most uncertainty-aware robotic systems collapse prediction uncertainty into a single scalar score and use it to trigger uniform corrective responses. This aggregation obscures whether uncertainty arises from corrupted observations or from mismatch between the learned model and the true system dynamics. As a result, corrective actions may be applied to the wrong component of the closed loop, degrading performance relative to leaving the policy unchanged. We introduce a lightweight post hoc framework that decomposes uncertainty into aleatoric and epistemic components and uses these signals to regulate system responses at inference time. Aleatoric uncertainty is estimated from deviations in the observation distribution using a Mahalanobis density model, while epistemic uncertainty is detected using a noise robust forward dynamics ensemble that isolates model mismatch from measurement corruption. The two signals remain empirically near orthogonal during closed loop execution and enable type specific responses. High aleatoric uncertainty triggers observation recovery, while high epistemic uncertainty moderates control actions. The same signals also regulate adaptive perception by guiding model capacity selection during tracking inference. Experiments demonstrate consistent improvements across both control and perception tasks. In robotic manipulation, the decomposed controller improves task success from 59.4% to 80.4% under compound perturbations and outperforms a combined uncertainty baseline by up to 21.0%. In adaptive tracking inference on MOT17, uncertainty-guided model selection reduces average compute by 58.2% relative to a fixed high capacity detector while preserving detection quality within 0.4%. Code and demo videos are available at https://divake.github.io/uncertainty-decomposition/.

</details>


### [467] [UniGround: Universal 3D Visual Grounding via Training-Free Scene Parsing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.08131)
*Jiaxi Zhang,Yunheng Wang,Wei Lu,Taowen Wang,Weisheng Xu,Shuning Zhang,Yixiao Feng,Yuetong Fang,Renjing Xu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: UniGround introduces a training-free approach for open-world 3D visual grounding that uses visual and geometric reasoning to locate any object in any scene without 3D supervision, achieving state-of-the-art zero-shot performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current 3D visual grounding methods rely on pre-trained models that are constrained by their training data, limiting generalization to unseen spatial relationships and robustness to out-of-distribution scenes.

Method: Two-stage approach: 1) Global Candidate Filtering using training-free 3D topology and multi-view semantic encoding, and 2) Local Precision Grounding using multi-scale visual prompting and structured reasoning.

Result: Achieves 46.1%/34.1% Acc@0.25/0.5 on ScanRefer and 28.7% Acc@0.25 on EmbodiedScan, establishing new SOTA among zero-shot methods without any 3D supervision. Shows robust generalization in real-world environments.

Conclusion: Training-free visual and geometric reasoning enables open-world 3D visual grounding that generalizes beyond curated benchmarks and inherited knowledge boundaries of pre-trained models.

Abstract: Understanding and localizing objects in complex 3D environments from natural language descriptions, known as 3D Visual Grounding (3DVG), is a foundational challenge in embodied AI, with broad implications for robotics, augmented reality, and human-machine interaction. Large-scale pre-trained foundation models have driven significant progress on this front, enabling open-vocabulary 3DVG that allows systems to locate arbitrary objects in a given scene. However, their reliance on pre-trained models constrains 3D perception and reasoning within the inherited knowledge boundaries, resulting in limited generalization to unseen spatial relationships and poor robustness to out-of-distribution scenes. In this paper, we replace this constrained perception with training-free visual and geometric reasoning, thereby unlocking open-world 3DVG that enables the localization of any object in any scene beyond the training data. Specifically, the proposed UniGround operates in two stages: a Global Candidate Filtering stage that constructs scene candidates through training-free 3D topology and multi-view semantic encoding, and a Local Precision Grounding stage that leverages multi-scale visual prompting and structured reasoning to precisely identify the target object. Experiments on ScanRefer and EmbodiedScan show that UniGround achieves 46.1\%/34.1\% Acc@0.25/0.5 on ScanRefer and 28.7\% Acc@0.25 on EmbodiedScan, establishing a new state-of-the-art among zero-shot methods on EmbodiedScan without any 3D supervision. We further evaluate UniGround in real-world environments under uncontrolled reconstruction conditions and substantial domain shift, showing training-free reasoning generalizes robustly beyond curated benchmarks.

</details>


### [468] [POIROT: Investigating Direct Tangible vs. Digitally Mediated Interaction and Attitude Moderation in Multi-party Murder Mystery Games](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.08136)
*Wen Chen,Rongxi Chen,Shankai Chen,Huiyang Gong,Minghui Guo,Yingri Xu,Xintong Wu,Xinyi Fu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Physical robot interaction isn't universally better - users' negative attitudes toward robots moderate whether tangible or digital interfaces work best for engagement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To challenge the assumption that physical/tangible interaction always enhances user experience with social robots, and identify boundary conditions based on users' attitudes toward robots.

Method: Between-subjects experiment (N=67) with custom-built robot game master facilitating murder mystery game. Compared tangible interaction vs. digitally mediated interface for clue delivery. Used MANOVA and multilevel linear models to analyze effects of modality and NARS (Negative Attitudes towards Robots) scores.

Result: No main effect of delivery modality on engagement, but significant moderation by NARS: high-NARS users had lower narrative immersion with tangible delivery, while low-NARS users showed no difference. Qualitative data showed tangibility creates proxemic friction for anxious users but provides novelty for others.

Conclusion: Tangible interaction isn't universally superior; user predispositions (especially NARS) moderate effectiveness. Need adaptive systems that tailor interaction modalities to individual user characteristics rather than one-size-fits-all approaches.

Abstract: As social robots take on increasingly complex roles like game masters (GMs) in multi-party games, the expectation that physicality universally enhances user experience remains debated. This study challenges the "one-size-fits-all" view of tangible interaction by identifying a critical boundary condition: users' Negative Attitudes towards Robots (NARS). In a between-subjects experiment (N = 67), a custom-built robot GM facilitated a multi-party murder mystery game (MMG) by delivering clues either through direct tangible interaction or a digitally mediated interface. Baseline multivariate analysis (MANOVA) showed no significant main effect of delivery modality, confirming that tangibility alone does not guarantee superior engagement. However, primary analysis using multilevel linear models (MLM) revealed a reliable moderation: participants high in NARS experienced markedly lower narrative immersion under tangible delivery, whereas those with low NARS scores showed no such decrement. Qualitative findings further illuminate this divergence: tangibility provides novelty and engagement for some but imposes excessive proxemic friction for anxious users, for whom the digital interface acts as a protective social buffer. These results advance a conditional model of HRI and emphasize the necessity for adaptive systems that can tailor interaction modalities to user predispositions.

</details>


### [469] [Multifingered force-aware control for humanoid robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.08142)
*Pasquale Marra,Gabriele M. Caddeo,Ugo Pattacini,Lorenzo Natale*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Force-aware control for multi-fingered robotic hands using tactile sensor force estimates to adapt motion and redistribute forces for stable object contact.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need for robotic platforms with multi-fingered hands to maintain stable contact with objects of varying mass distribution or unstable contacts using force-aware control.

Method: Collect tactile dataset with Xela magnetic sensors, train force estimators, then implement model-based control minimizing distance between Center of Pressure and fingertip contact polygon centroid.

Result: Achieved 82.7% success rate on balancing task with five objects and 80% accuracy in multi-object scenarios.

Conclusion: Force estimation-based approach enables effective force redistribution for stable manipulation and can generalize to any sensor capable of force estimation.

Abstract: In this paper, we address force-aware control and force distribution in robotic platforms with multi-fingered hands. Given a target goal and force estimates from tactile sensors, we design a controller that adapts the motion of the torso, arm, wrist, and fingers, redistributing forces to maintain stable contact with objects of varying mass distribution or unstable contacts. To estimate forces, we collect a dataset of tactile signals and ground-truth force measurements using five Xela magnetic sensors interacting with indenters, and train force estimators. We then introduce a model-based control scheme that minimizes the distance between the Center of Pressure (CoP) and the centroid of the fingertips contact polygon. Since our method relies on estimated forces rather than raw tactile signals, it has the potential to be applied to any sensor capable of force estimation. We validate our framework on a balancing task with five objects, achieving a $82.7\%$ success rate, and further evaluate it in multi-object scenarios, achieving $80\%$ accuracy. Code and data can be found here https://github.com/hsp-iit/multifingered-force-aware-control.

</details>


### [470] [A General Lie-Group Framework for Continuum Soft Robot Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.08232)
*Lingxiao Xun,Benoît Rosa,Jérôme Szewczyk,Brahim Tamadazte*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A Lie group framework using SE(3) for continuum soft robot modeling that unifies kinematics, statics, and dynamics with geometric local control and efficient computation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome limitations in existing strain-based and configuration-based methods for continuum soft robots, particularly addressing issues with geometric local control and unit quaternion constraints, while providing a unified modeling approach.

Method: Combines Cosserat rod theory with cumulative parameterization on the Lie group SE(3), deriving unified analytical expressions for kinematics, statics, and dynamics, including recursive Jacobian computations and an energy-conserving integrator for real-time simulation.

Result: The framework successfully models various complex robotic structures (segmented, branched, nested, rigid-soft composites) and demonstrates effectiveness in scenarios including large-deformation rods, concentric tube robots, parallel robots, cable-driven robots, and articulated fingers.

Conclusion: The proposed Lie group framework enhances modeling flexibility and numerical performance, providing an improved toolset for designing, simulating, and controlling soft robotic systems with computational efficiency suitable for real-time applications.

Abstract: This paper introduces a general Lie group framework for modeling continuum soft robots, employing Cosserat rod theory combined with cumulative parameterization on the Lie group SE(3). This novel approach addresses limitations present in current strain-based and configuration-based methods by providing geometric local control and eliminating unit quaternion constraints. The paper derives unified analytical expressions for kinematics, statics, and dynamics, including recursive Jacobian computations and an energy-conserving integrator suitable for real-time simulation and control. Additionally, the framework is extended to handle complex robotic structures, including segmented, branched, nested, and rigid-soft composite configurations, facilitating a modular and unified modeling strategy. The effectiveness, generality, and computational efficiency of the proposed methodology are demonstrated through various scenarios, including large-deformation rods, concentric tube robots, parallel robots, cable-driven robots, and articulated fingers. This work enhances modeling flexibility and numerical performance, providing an improved toolset for designing, simulating, and controlling soft robotic systems.

</details>


### [471] [FlowTouch: View-Invariant Visuo-Tactile Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.08255)
*Seongjin Bien,Carlo Kneissl,Tobias Jülg,Frank Fundel,Thomas Ressler-Antal,Florian Walter,Björn Ommer,Gitta Kutyniok,Wolfram Burgard*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: FlowTouch: A view-invariant visuo-tactile prediction model that uses object 3D meshes to predict tactile patterns, bridging the gap between visual planning and tactile sensing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tactile sensors only provide readings during contact, limiting their use in planning and initial task execution. Predicting tactile information from vision can bridge this gap, but existing methods depend heavily on specific camera setups and viewing angles.

Method: Uses object's local 3D mesh to encode rich information for predicting tactile patterns while abstracting away scene-dependent details. Integrates scene reconstruction with Flow Matching-based models for image generation to achieve view-invariant prediction.

Result: FlowTouch successfully bridges the sim-to-real gap and generalizes to new sensor instances. The generated tactile images can be effectively used for downstream tasks like grasp stability prediction.

Conclusion: The proposed FlowTouch model provides a robust, view-invariant approach for predicting tactile information from visual data, enabling better integration of tactile sensing into robotic manipulation tasks from planning through execution.

Abstract: Tactile sensation is essential for contact-rich manipulation tasks. It provides direct feedback on object geometry, surface properties, and interaction forces, enhancing perception and enabling fine-grained control. An inherent limitation of tactile sensors is that readings are available only when an object is touched. This precludes their use during planning and the initial execution phase of a task. Predicting tactile information from visual information can bridge this gap. A common approach is to learn a direct mapping from camera images to the output of vision-based tactile sensors. However, the resulting model will depend strongly on the specific setup and on how well the camera can capture the area where an object is touched. In this work, we introduce FlowTouch, a novel model for view-invariant visuo-tactile prediction. Our key idea is to use an object's local 3D mesh to encode rich information for predicting tactile patterns while abstracting away from scene-dependent details. FlowTouch integrates scene reconstruction and Flow Matching-based models for image generation. Our results show that FlowTouch is able to bridge the sim-to-real gap and generalize to new sensor instances. We further show that the resulting tactile images can be used for downstream grasp stability prediction. Our code, datasets and videos are available at https://flowtouch.github.io/

</details>


### [472] [Seed2Scale: A Self-Evolving Data Engine for Embodied AI via Small to Large Model Synergy and Multimodal Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.08260)
*Cong Tai,Zhaoyu Zheng,Haixu Long,Hansheng Wu,Zhengbin Long,Haodong Xiang,Rong Shi,Zhuo Cui,Shizhuang Zhang,Gang Qiu,He Wang,Ruifeng Li,Biao Liu,Zhenzhe Sun,Tao Shen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Seed2Scale is a self-evolving data engine that uses small models for exploration, large models for evaluation, and target models for learning to overcome data bottlenecks in embodied AI, achieving 131.2% performance improvement with minimal seed data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing data generation methods suffer from exploration limits, embodiment gaps, and low signal-to-noise ratios, causing performance degradation during self-iteration. There's a need to overcome data bottlenecks for scalable development of Generalist Embodied AI.

Method: Seed2Scale uses a heterogeneous synergy approach: 1) SuperTiny (lightweight Vision-Language-Action model) as dedicated collector for robust exploration in parallel environments, 2) pre-trained Vision-Language Model as Verifier for autonomous success/failure judgment and quality scoring, and 3) target-model learning. The system starts with only 4 seed demonstrations and mitigates model collapse.

Result: Seed2Scale shows significant scaling potential with robust upward trend in success rate as iterations progress, achieving 131.2% performance improvement. It significantly outperforms existing data augmentation methods and provides scalable, cost-effective pathway for Generalist Embodied AI development.

Conclusion: Seed2Scale effectively addresses data bottleneck challenges through its self-evolving data engine architecture, demonstrating superior performance and scalability compared to existing methods while ensuring stability through mitigation of model collapse.

Abstract: Existing data generation methods suffer from exploration limits, embodiment gaps, and low signal-to-noise ratios, leading to performance degradation during self-iteration. To address these challenges, we propose Seed2Scale, a self-evolving data engine that overcomes the data bottleneck through a heterogeneous synergy of "small-model collection, large-model evaluation, and target-model learning". Starting with as few as four seed demonstrations, the engine employs the lightweight Vision-Language-Action model, SuperTiny, as a dedicated collector, leveraging its strong inductive bias for robust exploration in parallel environments. Concurrently, a pre-trained Vision-Language Model is integrated as a Verifer to autonomously perform success/failure judgment and quality scoring for the massive generated trajectories. Seed2Scale effectively mitigates model collapse, ensuring the stability of the self-evolution process. Experimental results demonstrate that Seed2Scale exhibits signifcant scaling potential: as iterations progress, the success rate of the target model shows a robust upward trend, achieving a performance improvement of 131.2%. Furthermore, Seed2Scale signifcantly outperforms existing data augmentation methods, providing a scalable and cost-effective pathway for the large-scale development of Generalist Embodied AI. Project page: https://terminators2025.github.io/Seed2Scale.github.io

</details>


### [473] [SAIL: Test-Time Scaling for In-Context Imitation Learning with VLM](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.08269)
*Makoto Sato,Yusuke Iwasawa,Yujin Tang,So Kuroki*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SAIL reframes robot imitation as iterative refinement using Monte Carlo Tree Search with trajectory archives, VLM scoring, and step-level feedback to improve success rates with more test-time compute.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: One-shot trajectory generation for in-context imitation learning is fragile under environmental variation, limiting robot skill acquisition from demonstrations.

Method: SAIL uses Monte Carlo Tree Search where nodes are complete trajectories and edges are refinements, with three components: automated archive for relevant trajectory retrieval, VLM-based scoring for evaluation, and step-level feedback for iterative refinement.

Result: Increasing test-time compute consistently improves success rates across six diverse manipulation tasks, achieving up to 95% on complex tasks in simulation and real-world validation.

Conclusion: Trajectory-level test-time scaling is a robust path toward more generalizable robotic agents that can adapt to environmental variations.

Abstract: In-context imitation learning allows robots to acquire skills from demonstrations, yet one-shot trajectory generation remains fragile under environmental variation. We propose SAIL, a framework that reframes robot imitation as an iterative refinement problem capable of scaling with test-time compute. SAIL utilizes Monte Carlo Tree Search, where each node is a complete trajectory and edges correspond to trajectory refinements. The process is guided by three core components: an automated archive of successful trajectories for contextually relevant retrieval, a vision language model-based scoring mechanism for trajectory evaluation, and a step-level feedback that provides trajectory-aligned scores for iterative refinement. Experiments across six diverse manipulation tasks in simulation and real-world validation clearly demonstrate that increasing test-time compute consistently improves success rates, achieving up to 95% on complex tasks. Our results suggest that trajectory-level test-time scaling is a robust path toward more generalizable robotic agents.

</details>


### [474] [Less is More: Robust Zero-Communication 3D Pursuit-Evasion via Representational Parsimony](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.08273)
*Jialin Ying,Zhihao Li,Zicheng Dong,Guohua Wu,Yihuan Liao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Parsimonious observation interface (83-D to 50-D) with Contribution-Gated Credit Assignment improves communication-free 3D pursuit-evasion coordination under latency and partial observability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Asymmetric 3D pursuit-evasion in cluttered environments is challenging due to communication latency, partial observability, and nonholonomic constraints. Traditional MARL methods rely on rich inter-agent coupling or centralized signals, which become fragility sources when communication is delayed or noisy.

Method: Two key innovations: (1) Parsimonious actor observation interface that removes team-coupled channels (reducing from 83-D to 50-D), and (2) Contribution-Gated Credit Assignment (CGCA), a locality-aware credit structure for communication-denied cooperation.

Result: In Stage-5 evaluation (4 pursuers vs. 1 evader), achieves 0.753 +/- 0.091 success rate and 0.223 +/- 0.066 collision rate, outperforming the 83-D FULL OBS counterpart (0.721 +/- 0.071, 0.253 +/- 0.089). Shows graceful degradation under stress tests and resilient zero-shot transfer on urban-canyon maps (~61% success at density 0.24).

Conclusion: Explicitly severing redundant cross-agent channels can suppress compounding error cascades and improve robustness in latency-prone deployments, supporting a practical paradigm shift toward representational parsimony for communication-free coordination.

Abstract: Asymmetric 3D pursuit-evasion in cluttered voxel environments is difficult under communication latency, partial observability, and nonholonomic maneuver limits. While many MARL methods rely on richer inter-agent coupling or centralized signals, these dependencies can become fragility sources when communication is delayed or noisy. Building on an inherited path-guided decentralized pursuit scaffold, we study a robustness-oriented question: can representational parsimony improve communication-free coordination? We instantiate this principle with (i) a parsimonious actor observation interface that removes team-coupled channels (83-D to 50-D), and (ii) Contribution-Gated Credit Assignment (CGCA), a locality-aware credit structure for communication-denied cooperation. In Stage-5 evaluation (4 pursuers vs. 1 evader), our configuration reaches 0.753 +/- 0.091 success and 0.223 +/- 0.066 collision, outperforming the 83-D FULL OBS counterpart (0.721 +/- 0.071, 0.253 +/- 0.089). It further shows graceful degradation under speed/yaw/noise/delay stress tests and resilient zero-shot transfer on urban-canyon maps (about 61% success at density 0.24). These results support a practical paradigm shift: explicitly severing redundant cross-agent channels can suppress compounding error cascades and improve robustness in latency-prone deployment.

</details>


### [475] [EndoSERV: A Vision-based Endoluminal Robot Navigation System](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.08324)
*Junyang Wu,Fangfang Xie,Minghui Zhang,Hanxiao Zhang,Jiayuan Sun,Yun Gu,Guang-Zhong Yang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: EndoSERV: A novel vision-based localization method for robot-assisted endoluminal procedures that combines segment-to-structure mapping and real-to-virtual feature transfer to enable accurate navigation without real pose labels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robot-assisted endoluminal procedures face navigation challenges due to intricate, narrow, tortuous pathways. Vision-based navigation is promising but existing localization methods are error-prone due to tissue deformation, in vivo artifacts, and lack of distinctive landmarks for consistent localization.

Method: EndoSERV includes two main parts: 1) Segment-to-structure mapping for long-range complex luminal structures by dividing them into smaller sub-segments and estimating odometry independently, and 2) Real-to-virtual mapping using an efficient transfer technique to map real image features to virtual domain to utilize virtual pose ground truth. Training includes offline pretraining to extract texture-agnostic features and online adaptation to real-world conditions.

Result: Extensive experiments based on both public and clinical datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the method even without any real pose labels.

Conclusion: EndoSERV provides a novel solution for accurate vision-based localization in robot-assisted endoluminal procedures by addressing challenges of tissue deformation, artifacts, and landmark scarcity through segment-based mapping and virtual domain transfer learning.

Abstract: Robot-assisted endoluminal procedures are increasingly used for early cancer intervention. However, the intricate, narrow and tortuous pathways within the luminal anatomy pose substantial difficulties for robot navigation. Vision-based navigation offers a promising solution, but existing localization approaches are error-prone due to tissue deformation, in vivo artifacts and a lack of distinctive landmarks for consistent localization. This paper presents a novel EndoSERV localization method to address these challenges. It includes two main parts, \textit{i.e.}, \textbf{SE}gment-to-structure and \textbf{R}eal-to-\textbf{V}irtual mapping, and hence the name. For long-range and complex luminal structures, we divide them into smaller sub-segments and estimate the odometry independently. To cater for label insufficiency, an efficient transfer technique maps real image features to the virtual domain to use virtual pose ground truth. The training phases of EndoSERV include an offline pretraining to extract texture-agnostic features, and an online phase that adapts to real-world conditions. Extensive experiments based on both public and clinical datasets have been performed to demonstrate the effectiveness of the method even without any real pose labels.

</details>


### [476] [Hierarchical Multi-Modal Planning for Fixed-Altitude Sparse Target Search and Sampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.08336)
*Lingpeng Chen,Yuchen Zheng,Apple Pui-Yi Chui,Junfeng Wu,Ziyang Hong*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: HIMoS is a fixed-altitude hierarchical planning framework for efficient sparse coral monitoring using multi-modal sensing without costly vertical maneuvers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional AUV coral monitoring methods are energy-inefficient (exhaustive coverage) or require costly vertical maneuvers (adaptive sampling), creating a need for more efficient sparse benthic phenomena monitoring.

Method: HIMoS integrates heterogeneous sensors in a two-layer architecture: Global Planner optimizes topological routes for discovery potential, and Local Planner uses differentiable belief propagation to generate kinematically feasible trajectories balancing acoustic substrate exploration, visual coral search, and close-range sampling.

Result: Validated in high-fidelity simulations from real-world coral reef surveys, HIMoS demonstrates superior mission efficiency compared to state-of-the-art baselines.

Conclusion: HIMoS provides an effective fixed-altitude framework for sparse coral search-and-sample missions that overcomes limitations of traditional approaches while maintaining efficiency.

Abstract: Efficient monitoring of sparse benthic phenomena, such as coral colonies, presents a great challenge for Autonomous Underwater Vehicles. Traditional exhaustive coverage strategies are energy-inefficient, while recent adaptive sampling approaches rely on costly vertical maneuvers. To address these limitations, we propose HIMoS (Hierarchical Informative Multi-Modal Search), a fixed-altitude framework for sparse coral search-and-sample missions. The system integrates a heterogeneous sensor suite within a two-layer planning architecture. At the strategic level, a Global Planner optimizes topological routes to maximize potential discovery. At the tactical level, a receding-horizon Local Planner leverages differentiable belief propagation to generate kinematically feasible trajectories that balance acoustic substrate exploration, visual coral search, and close-range sampling. Validated in high-fidelity simulations derived from real-world coral reef benthic surveys, our approach demonstrates superior mission efficiency compared to state-of-the-art baselines.

</details>


### [477] [PhaForce: Phase-Scheduled Visual-Force Policy Learning with Slow Planning and Fast Correction for Contact-Rich Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.08342)
*Mingxin Wang,Zhirun Yue,Renhao Lu,Yizhe Li,Zihan Wang,Guoping Pan,Kangkang Dong,Jun Cheng,Yi Cheng,Houde Liu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: PhaForce is a phase-scheduled visual-force policy that coordinates low-rate planning with high-rate force corrections for contact-rich manipulation tasks, achieving 86% success rate (+40pp over baselines).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current visuomotor policies have low-frequency updates that underutilize force/torque feedback, and existing force-aware methods lack explicit mechanisms to schedule when/how much/where to apply force across different task phases.

Method: Three-component system: 1) Contact-aware phase predictor estimates contact probability and phase belief, 2) Slow diffusion planner performs dual-gated visual-force fusion with orthogonal residual injection, 3) Fast corrector applies control-rate phase-routed residuals in interpretable corrective subspaces.

Result: Achieves 86% average success rate (+40 percentage points over baselines), improves contact quality by regulating interaction forces, and exhibits robust adaptability to out-of-distribution geometric shifts.

Conclusion: PhaForce effectively coordinates low-rate planning with high-rate force corrections through phase scheduling, significantly improving performance in contact-rich manipulation tasks while maintaining vision semantics and interpretable corrective actions.

Abstract: Contact-rich manipulation requires not only vision-dominant task semantics but also closed-loop reactions to force/torque (F/T) transients. Yet, generative visuomotor policies are typically constrained to low-frequency updates due to inference latency and action chunking, underutilizing F/T for control-rate feedback. Furthermore, existing force-aware methods often inject force continuously and indiscriminately, lacking an explicit mechanism to schedule when / how much / where to apply force across different task phases. We propose PhaForce, a phase-scheduled visual--force policy that coordinates low-rate chunk-level planning and high-rate residual correction via a unified contact/phase schedule. PhaForce comprises (i) a contact-aware phase predictor (CAP) that estimates contact probability and phase belief, (ii) a Slow diffusion planner that performs dual-gated visual--force fusion with orthogonal residual injection to preserve vision semantics while conditioning on force, and (iii) a Fast corrector that applies control-rate phase-routed residuals in interpretable corrective subspaces for within-chunk micro-adjustments. Across multiple real-robot contact-rich tasks, PhaForce achieves an average success rate of 86% (+40 pp over baselines), while also substantially improving contact quality by regulating interaction forces and exhibiting robust adaptability to OOD geometric shifts.

</details>


### [478] [Perception-Aware Communication-Free Multi-UAV Coordination in the Wild](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.08379)
*Manuel Boldrer,Michal Kamler,Afzal Ahmad,Martin Saska*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Communication-free multi-robot coordination using 3D LiDAR for SLAM and neighbor detection in GNSS-denied environments like forests, with perception-aware navigation for safe goal progression.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-robot coordination in complex environments like forests with dense canopy cover where GNSS is unavailable, requiring communication-free operation and robust navigation despite limited sensor field-of-view.

Method: Uses onboard anisotropic 3D LiDAR for simultaneous SLAM, obstacle detection, and neighbor robot detection. Develops a novel perception-aware 3D navigation framework that enables safe progress toward goals despite limited sensor visibility.

Result: Evaluated through extensive simulations across diverse scenarios and validated in real-world field experiments, demonstrating scalability, robustness, and reliability of the approach.

Conclusion: The communication-free method using 3D LiDAR enables safe multi-robot coordination in GNSS-denied complex environments, with perception-aware navigation effectively addressing limited sensor field-of-view challenges.

Abstract: We present a communication-free method for safe multi-robot coordination in complex environments such as forests with dense canopy cover, where GNSS is unavailable. Our approach relies on an onboard anisotropic 3D LiDAR sensor used for SLAM as well as for detecting obstacles and neighboring robots. We develop a novel perception-aware 3D navigation framework that enables robots to safely and effectively progress toward a goal region despite limited sensor field-of-view. The approach is evaluated through extensive simulations across diverse scenarios and validated in real-world field experiments, demonstrating its scalability, robustness, and reliability.

</details>


### [479] [MoMaStage: Skill-State Graph Guided Planning and Closed-Loop Execution for Long-Horizon Indoor Mobile Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.08383)
*Chenxu Li,Zixuan Chen,Yetao Li,Jiapeng Xu,Hongyu Ding,Jieqi Shi,Jing Huo,Yang Gao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: MoMaStage: A structured vision-language framework for long-horizon indoor mobile manipulation that eliminates explicit scene mapping by grounding VLMs in hierarchical skills and topology-aware graphs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current indoor mobile manipulation systems struggle with long-horizon execution due to cascading errors and limited generalization. Learning-based approaches fail to maintain logical consistency over extended horizons, while explicit scene representation methods impose rigid structural assumptions that reduce adaptability in dynamic environments.

Method: MoMaStage grounds a Vision-Language Model within a Hierarchical Skill Library and a topology-aware Skill-State Graph, constraining task decomposition and skill composition within a feasible transition space. It incorporates closed-loop execution with proprioceptive feedback monitoring and triggers graph-constrained semantic replanning when deviations are detected.

Result: Extensive experiments in physics-rich simulations and real-world environments show MoMaStage outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving substantially higher planning success, reducing token overhead, and significantly improving overall task success rates in long-horizon mobile manipulation.

Conclusion: MoMaStage provides a structured vision-language framework that eliminates the need for explicit scene mapping while ensuring logical consistency and topological validity, enabling robust long-horizon mobile manipulation through hierarchical skill grounding and closed-loop execution.

Abstract: Indoor mobile manipulation (MoMA) enables robots to translate natural language instructions into physical actions, yet long-horizon execution remains challenging due to cascading errors and limited generalization across diverse environments. Learning-based approaches often fail to maintain logical consistency over extended horizons, while methods relying on explicit scene representations impose rigid structural assumptions that reduce adaptability in dynamic settings. To address these limitations, we propose MoMaStage, a structured vision-language framework for long-horizon MoMA that eliminates the need for explicit scene mapping. MoMaStage grounds a Vision-Language Model (VLM) within a Hierarchical Skill Library and a topology-aware Skill-State Graph, constraining task decomposition and skill composition within a feasible transition space. This structured grounding ensures that generated plans remain logically consistent and topologically valid with respect to the agent's evolving physical state. To enhance robustness, MoMaStage incorporates a closed-loop execution mechanism that monitors proprioceptive feedback and triggers graph-constrained semantic replanning when deviations are detected, maintaining alignment between planned skills and physical outcomes. Extensive experiments in physics-rich simulations and real-world environments demonstrate that MoMaStage outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving substantially higher planning success, reducing token overhead, and significantly improving overall task success rates in long-horizon mobile manipulation. Video demonstrations are available on the project website: https://chenxuli-cxli.github.io/MoMaStage/.

</details>


### [480] [StructBiHOI: Structured Articulation Modeling for Long--Horizon Bimanual Hand--Object Interaction Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.08390)
*Zhi Wang,Liu Liu,Ruonan Liu,Dan Guo,Meng Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: StructBiHOI: A hierarchical framework for generating long-horizon bimanual hand-object interactions using structured articulation modeling with jointVAE for long-term planning and maniVAE for frame-level refinement, enhanced by Mamba-based diffusion for efficient long-sequence generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing 3D hand-object interaction generation methods focus on single-hand grasps, but bimanual manipulation is much more challenging due to long-horizon planning instability, fine-grained joint articulation, and complex cross-hand coordination. Current approaches struggle with temporal consistency, physical plausibility, and semantic alignment over extended sequences.

Method: Proposes StructBiHOI with structured articulation modeling that disentangles temporal joint planning from frame-level manipulation refinement. Uses jointVAE for long-term joint evolution conditioned on object geometry and task semantics, and maniVAE for fine-grained hand pose refinement at single-frame level. Incorporates state-space-inspired diffusion denoiser based on Mamba for efficient long-range dependency modeling with linear complexity.

Result: Extensive experiments on bimanual manipulation and single-hand grasping benchmarks show superior long-horizon stability, motion realism, and computational efficiency compared to strong baselines.

Conclusion: StructBiHOI's hierarchical design facilitates coherent dual-hand coordination and articulated object interaction, effectively addressing the challenges of long-horizon bimanual hand-object interaction generation.

Abstract: Recent progress in 3D hand--object interaction (HOI) generation has primarily focused on single--hand grasp synthesis, while bimanual manipulation remains significantly more challenging. Long--horizon planning instability, fine--grained joint articulation, and complex cross--hand coordination make coherent bimanual generation difficult, especially under multimodal conditions. Existing approaches often struggle to simultaneously ensure temporal consistency, physical plausibility, and semantic alignment over extended sequences. We propose StructBiHOI, a Structured articulation modeling framework for long-horizon Bimanual HOI generation. Our key insight is to structurally disentangle temporal joint planning from frame--level manipulation refinement. Specifically, a jointVAE models long-term joint evolution conditioned on object geometry and task semantics, while a maniVAE refines fine-grained hand poses at the single--frame level. To enable stable and efficient long--sequence generation, we incorporate a state--space--inspired diffusion denoiser based on Mamba, which models long--range dependencies with linear complexity. This hierarchical design facilitates coherent dual-hand coordination and articulated object interaction. Extensive experiments on bimanual manipulation and single-hand grasping benchmarks demonstrate that our method achieves superior long--horizon stability, motion realism, and computational efficiency compared to strong baselines.

</details>


### [481] [Human-Aware Robot Behaviour in Self-Driving Labs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.08420)
*Satheeshkumar Veeramani,Anna Kisil,Abigail Bentley,Hatem Fakhruldeen,Gabriella Pizzuto,Andrew I. Cooper*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: AI-driven perception system enables mobile robot chemists to proactively interact with humans in shared labs by predicting human intentions, reducing delays from passive waiting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current mobile robot chemists in self-driving labs rely on simple LiDAR-based obstruction detection that forces passive waiting when humans are present, causing unnecessary delays and inefficient coordination in time-critical automated workflows in human-robot shared environments.

Method: Embodied, AI-driven perception method featuring a hierarchical human intention prediction model that allows robots to distinguish between preparatory actions (waiting) and transient interactions (accessing instruments).

Result: The proposed approach enhances efficiency by enabling proactive human-robot interaction, streamlining coordination, and potentially increasing the efficiency of autonomous scientific labs.

Conclusion: AI-driven perception with human intention prediction enables more efficient human-robot coordination in shared-access self-driving laboratories, moving beyond passive waiting to proactive interaction.

Abstract: Self-driving laboratories (SDLs) are rapidly transforming research in chemistry and materials science to accelerate new discoveries. Mobile robot chemists (MRCs) play a pivotal role by autonomously navigating the lab to transport samples, effectively connecting synthesis, analysis, and characterisation equipment. The instruments within an SDL are typically designed or retrofitted to be accessed by both human and robotic chemists, ensuring operational flexibility and integration between manual and automated workflows. In many scenarios, human and robotic chemists may need to use the same equipment simultaneously. Currently, MRCs rely on simple LiDAR-based obstruction detection, which forces the robot to passively wait if a human is present. This lack of situational awareness leads to unnecessary delays and inefficient coordination in time-critical automated workflows in human-robot shared labs. To address this, we present an initial study of an embodied, AI-driven perception method that facilitates proactive human-robot interaction in shared-access scenarios. Our method features a hierarchical human intention prediction model that allows the robot to distinguish between preparatory actions (waiting) and transient interactions (accessing the instrument). Our results demonstrate that the proposed approach enhances efficiency by enabling proactive human-robot interaction, streamlining coordination, and potentially increasing the efficiency of autonomous scientific labs.

</details>


### [482] [Tactile Recognition of Both Shapes and Materials with Automatic Feature Optimization-Enabled Meta Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.08423)
*Hongliang Zhao,Wenhui Yang,Yang Chen,Zhuorui Wang,Baiheng Liu,Longhui Qin*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: AFOP-ML framework uses meta-learning with automatic feature optimization for few-shot tactile recognition, achieving high accuracy with minimal training data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tactile perception is crucial for robotic manipulation but suffers from data scarcity and time-consuming training processes due to costly tactile data collection.

Method: Proposed AFOP-ML framework combining prototypical network with automatic feature optimization for meta-learning, using four-channel tactile signals to recognize shapes and materials.

Result: Achieved 96.08% accuracy in 5-way-1-shot scenario and 88.7% in extreme 36-way-1-shot case on 36-category benchmark, outperforming existing approaches with strong generalization to unseen conditions.

Conclusion: The framework enables rapid adaptation to new classes with minimal data while automatically optimizing feature space, providing insights for tactile recognition tasks and sensor design improvements.

Abstract: Tactile perception is indispensable for robots to implement various manipulations dexterously, especially in contact-rich scenarios. However, alongside the development of deep learning techniques, it meanwhile suffers from training data scarcity and a time-consuming learning process in practical applications since the collection of a large amount of tactile data is costly and sometimes even impossible. Hence, we propose an automatic feature optimization-enabled prototypical network to realize meta-learning, i.e., AFOP-ML framework. As a ``learn to learn" network, it not only adapts to new unseen classes rapidly with few-shot, but also learns how to determine the optimal feature space automatically. Based on the four-channel signals acquired from a tactile finger, both shapes and materials are recognized. On a 36-category benchmark, it outperforms several existing approaches by attaining an accuracy of 96.08% in 5-way-1-shot scenario, where only 1 example is available for training. It still remains 88.7% in the extreme 36-way-1-shot case. The generalization ability is further validated through three groups of experiment involving unseen shapes, materials and force/speed perturbations. More insights are additionally provided by this work for the interpretation of recognition tasks and improved design of tactile sensors.

</details>


### [483] [FoMo: A Multi-Season Dataset for Robot Navigation in Forêt Montmorency](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.08433)
*Matěj Boxan,Gabriel Jeanson,Alexander Krawciw,Effie Daum,Xinyuan Qiao,Sven Lilge,Timothy D. Barfoot,François Pomerleau*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: FoMo dataset is a comprehensive multi-season boreal forest dataset with 64km of diverse trajectories featuring snow accumulation, vegetation growth, and challenging conditions for odometry/SLAM evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a challenging dataset for evaluating odometry and SLAM algorithms in real-world boreal forest environments with significant seasonal changes that current methods struggle with.

Method: Collected data over one year with 12 deployments across 6 diverse trajectories using multiple sensors: two lidars (rotating and hybrid solid-state), FMCW radar, stereo and monocular cameras, two IMUs, and GNSS for ground truth.

Result: Dataset includes over 64km of trajectories with challenging conditions (snow >1m, vegetation growth, traction limits). Preliminary evaluation shows seasonal changes seriously affect re-localization capabilities of state-of-the-art methods.

Conclusion: The FoMo dataset provides a valuable benchmark for testing robustness of localization and mapping algorithms to seasonal environmental changes in challenging boreal forest conditions.

Abstract: The Forêt Montmorency (FoMo) dataset is a comprehensive multi-season data collection, recorded over the span of one year in a boreal forest. Featuring a unique combination of on- and off-pavement environments with significant environmental changes, the dataset challenges established odometry and SLAM pipelines. Some highlights of the data include the accumulation of snow exceeding 1 m, significant vegetation growth in front of sensors, and operations at the traction limits of the platform. In total, the FoMo dataset includes over 64 km of six diverse trajectories, repeated during 12 deployments throughout the year. The dataset features data from one rotating and one hybrid solid-state lidar, a Frequency Modulated Continuous Wave (FMCW) radar, full-HD images from a stereo camera and a wide lens monocular camera, as well as data from two IMUs. Ground Truth is calculated by post-processing three GNSS receivers mounted on the Uncrewed Ground Vehicle (UGV) and a static GNSS base station. Additional metadata, such as one measurement per minute from an on-site weather station, camera calibration intrinsics, and vehicle power consumption, is available for all sequences. To highlight the relevance of the dataset, we performed a preliminary evaluation of the robustness of a lidar-inertial, radar-gyro, and a visual-inertial localization and mapping techniques to seasonal changes. We show that seasonal changes have serious effects on the re-localization capabilities of the state-of-the-art methods. The dataset and development kit are available at https://fomo.norlab.ulaval.ca.

</details>


### [484] [Adaptive Entropy-Driven Sensor Selection in a Camera-LiDAR Particle Filter for Single-Vessel Tracking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.08457)
*Andrei Starodubov,Yaqub Aris Prabowo,Andreas Hadjipieris,Ioannis Kyriakides,Roberto Galeazzi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Multi-sensor fusion tracker with adaptive sensing policy for robust vessel tracking from coastal platforms, using LiDAR and camera with information-gain-based modality switching.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Single-sensor vessel tracking from fixed coastal platforms faces modality-specific limitations: cameras suffer from illumination issues and visual clutter, while LiDAR performance degrades with range and has intermittent returns.

Method: Heterogeneous multi-sensor fusion particle-filter tracker with information-gain (entropy-reduction) adaptive sensing policy that selects the most informative sensor configuration at each fusion time bin.

Result: LiDAR dominates near-field accuracy, camera sustains longer-range coverage when LiDAR becomes unavailable, and adaptive policy achieves favorable accuracy-continuity trade-off by switching modalities based on information gain.

Conclusion: Adaptive configuration provides practical baseline for resilient and resource-aware maritime surveillance by avoiding continuous multi-stream processing while maintaining tracking performance.

Abstract: Robust single-vessel tracking from fixed coastal platforms is hindered by modality-specific degradations: cameras suffer from illumination and visual clutter, while LiDAR performance drops with range and intermittent returns. We present a heterogeneous multi-sensor fusion particle-filter tracker that incorporates an information-gain (entropy-reduction) adaptive sensing policy to select the most informative configuration at each fusion time bin. The approach is validated in a real maritime deployment at the CMMI Smart Marina Testbed (Ayia Napa Marina, Cyprus), using a shore-mounted 3D LiDAR and an elevated fixed camera to track a rigid inflatable boat with onboard GNSS ground truth. We compare LiDAR-only, camera-only, all-sensors, and adaptive configurations. Results show LiDAR dominates near-field accuracy, the camera sustains longer-range coverage when LiDAR becomes unavailable, and the adaptive policy achieves a favorable accuracy-continuity trade-off by switching modalities based on information gain. By avoiding continuous multi-stream processing, the adaptive configuration provides a practical baseline for resilient and resource-aware maritime surveillance.

</details>


### [485] [R2F: Repurposing Ray Frontiers for LLM-free Object Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.08475)
*Francesco Argenziano,John Mark Alexis Marcelo,Michele Brienza,Abdel Hakim Drid,Emanuele Musumeci,Daniele Nardi,Domenico D. Bloisi,Vincenzo Suriani*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: R2F-VLN: LLM-free framework for real-time open-vocabulary object navigation using ray frontiers as semantic hypotheses, eliminating iterative large-model queries.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VLM/LLM-based navigation systems suffer from latency and computational overhead due to iterative large-model queries at inference time, limiting real-time deployment.

Method: Repurpose ray frontiers as explicit, direction-conditioned semantic hypotheses; store language-aligned features sparsely at frontiers; use embedding-based frontier scoring and goal tracking within classical mapping/planning pipeline; add lightweight syntactic parsing and relational verification for free-form instructions.

Result: Achieves competitive state-of-the-art zero-shot performance with real-time execution, up to 6× faster runtime than VLM-based alternatives in Habitat-sim and real robot experiments.

Conclusion: Demonstrates that effective open-vocabulary navigation can be achieved without iterative LLM/VLM queries, enabling real-time deployment through efficient frontier-based semantic reasoning.

Abstract: Zero-shot open-vocabulary object navigation has progressed rapidly with the emergence of large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) and Large Language Models (LLMs), now widely used as high-level decision-makers instead of end-to-end policies. Although effective, such systems often rely on iterative large-model queries at inference time, introducing latency and computational overhead that limit real-time deployment. To address this problem, we repurpose ray frontiers (R2F), a recently proposed frontier-based exploration paradigm, to develop an LLM-free framework for indoor open-vocabulary object navigation. While ray frontiers were originally used to bias exploration using semantic cues carried along rays, we reinterpret frontier regions as explicit, direction-conditioned semantic hypotheses that serve as navigation goals. Language-aligned features accumulated along out-of-range rays are stored sparsely at frontiers, where each region maintains multiple directional embeddings encoding plausible unseen content. In this way, navigation then reduces to embedding-based frontier scoring and goal tracking within a classical mapping and planning pipeline, eliminating iterative large-model reasoning. We further introduce R2F-VLN, a lightweight extension for free-form language instructions using syntactic parsing and relational verification without additional VLM or LLM components. Experiments in Habitat-sim and on a real robotic platform demonstrate competitive state-of-the-art zero-shot performance with real-time execution, achieving up to 6 times faster runtime than VLM-based alternatives.

</details>


### [486] [LAR-MoE: Latent-Aligned Routing for Mixture of Experts in Robotic Imitation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.08476)
*Ariel Rodriguez,Chenpan Li,Lorenzo Mazza,Rayan Younis,Ortrun Hellig,Sebastian Bodenstedt,Martin Wagner,Stefanie Speidel*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: LAR-MoE: A two-stage framework that decouples unsupervised skill discovery from policy learning for imitation learning, using latent-aligned routing to prevent expert collapse while maintaining parameter efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Imitation learning struggles with heterogeneous task dynamics where policies average over distinct behavioral modes; existing MoE architectures require meaningful skill decompositions for expert routing, which is challenging to obtain.

Method: Two-stage framework: 1) Pre-training learns joint latent representation between observations and future actions via student-teacher co-training; 2) Post-training regularizes expert routing to follow learned latent space structure, preventing expert collapse.

Result: Achieves 95.2% average success rate on LIBERO benchmark with 150M parameters; matches supervised MoE baseline on surgical bowel grasping/retraction without phase annotations; transfers zero-shot to ex vivo porcine tissue.

Conclusion: Latent-aligned routing provides principled alternative to supervised skill decomposition, enabling structured expert specialization from unlabeled demonstrations for improved imitation learning across heterogeneous tasks.

Abstract: Imitation learning enables robots to acquire manipulation skills from demonstrations, yet deploying a policy across tasks with heterogeneous dynamics remains challenging, as models tend to average over distinct behavioral modes present in the demonstrations. Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures address this by activating specialized subnetworks, but requires meaningful skill decompositions for expert routing. We introduce Latent-Aligned Routing for Mixture of Experts (LAR-MoE), a two-stage framework that decouples unsupervised skill discovery from policy learning. In pre-training, we learn a joint latent representation between observations and future actions through student-teacher co-training. In a post-training stage, the expert routing is regularized to follow the structure of the learned latent space, preventing expert collapse while maintaining parameter efficiency. We evaluate LAR-MoE in simulation and on hardware. On the LIBERO benchmark, our method achieves a 95.2% average success rate with 150M parameters. On a surgical bowel grasping and retraction task, LAR-MoE matches a supervised MoE baseline without requiring any phase annotations, and transfers zero-shot to ex vivo porcine tissue. Our findings suggest that latent-aligned routing provides a principled alternative to supervised skill decomposition, enabling structured expert specialization from unlabeled demonstrations.

</details>


### [487] [STRIDE: Structured Lagrangian and Stochastic Residual Dynamics via Flow Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.08478)
*Prakrut Kotecha,Ganga Nair B,Shishir Kolathaya*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: STRIDE: A dynamics learning framework that separates conservative rigid-body mechanics from stochastic non-conservative interactions using Lagrangian Neural Networks and Conditional Flow Matching for improved robotic system modeling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robotic systems in unstructured environments face significant uncertainty from intermittent contacts, friction variability, and unmodeled compliance. Existing approaches either use analytical rigid-body models that fail to capture complex interactions or purely data-driven models that violate physical consistency and accumulate drift.

Method: STRIDE separates conservative rigid-body mechanics from uncertain stochastic non-conservative interactions. Uses Lagrangian Neural Network (LNN) for energy-consistent inertial dynamics and Conditional Flow Matching (CFM) for multi-modal residual interaction forces. Both components are trained jointly end-to-end.

Result: 20% reduction in long-horizon prediction error and 30% reduction in contact force prediction error compared to deterministic residual baselines. Evaluated on pendulum, Unitree Go1 quadruped, and Unitree G1 humanoid systems.

Conclusion: STRIDE enables retention of physical structure while representing complex stochastic behavior, supporting more reliable model-based control in uncertain robotic environments by combining the strengths of physics-based and data-driven approaches.

Abstract: Robotic systems operating in unstructured environments must operate under significant uncertainty arising from intermittent contacts, frictional variability, and unmodeled compliance. While recent model-free approaches have demonstrated impressive performance, many deployment settings still require predictive models that support planning, constraint handling, and online adaptation. Analytical rigid-body models provide strong physical structure but often fail to capture complex interaction effects, whereas purely data-driven models may violate physical consistency, exhibit data bias, and accumulate long-horizon drift. In this work, we propose STRIDE, a dynamics learning framework that explicitly separates conservative rigid-body mechanics from uncertain, effectively stochastic non-conservative interaction effects. The structured component is modeled using a Lagrangian Neural Network (LNN) to preserve energy-consistent inertial dynamics, while residual interaction forces are represented using Conditional Flow Matching (CFM) to capture multi-modal interaction phenomena. The two components are trained jointly end-to-end, enabling the model to retain physical structure while representing complex stochastic behavior. We evaluate STRIDE on systems of increasing complexity, including a pendulum, the Unitree Go1 quadruped, and the Unitree G1 humanoid. Results show 20% reduction in long-horizon prediction error and 30% reduction in contact force prediction error compared to deterministic residual baselines, supporting more reliable model-based control in uncertain robotic environments.

</details>


### [488] [3PoinTr: 3D Point Tracks for Robot Manipulation Pretraining from Casual Videos](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.08485)
*Adam Hung,Bardienus Pieter Duisterhof,Jeffrey Ichnowski*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: 3PoinTr enables data-efficient robot policy training from casual human videos using 3D point tracks as embodiment-agnostic representation, achieving robust spatial generalization with only 20 demonstrations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current robot training requires large volumes of demonstrations, which is impractical. Learning from human videos is promising but faces embodiment gap challenges (differences in kinematics/strategies between humans and robots).

Method: Uses transformer architecture to predict 3D point tracks as intermediate embodiment-agnostic representation, encoding goal specifications, scene geometry, and spatiotemporal relationships. Employs Perceiver IO for compact representation extraction for sample-efficient behavior cloning.

Result: Achieves robust spatial generalization on diverse manipulation tasks with only 20 action-labeled robot demonstrations. Outperforms behavior cloning methods and prior human video pretraining methods. Produces more accurate 3D point tracks than existing baselines.

Conclusion: 3PoinTr enables data-efficient robot policy training from casual human videos by using 3D point tracks as embodiment-agnostic representation, overcoming the embodiment gap and reducing demonstration requirements.

Abstract: Data-efficient training of robust robot policies is the key to unlocking automation in a wide array of novel tasks. Current systems require large volumes of demonstrations to achieve robustness, which is impractical in many applications. Learning policies directly from human videos is a promising alternative that removes teleoperation costs, but it shifts the challenge toward overcoming the embodiment gap (differences in kinematics and strategies between robots and humans), often requiring restrictive and carefully choreographed human motions. We propose 3PoinTr, a method for pretraining robot policies from casual and unconstrained human videos, enabling learning from motions natural for humans. 3PoinTr uses a transformer architecture to predict 3D point tracks as an intermediate embodiment-agnostic representation. 3D point tracks encode goal specifications, scene geometry, and spatiotemporal relationships. We use a Perceiver IO architecture to extract a compact representation for sample-efficient behavior cloning, even when point tracks violate downstream embodiment-specific constraints. We conduct thorough evaluation on simulated and real-world tasks, and find that 3PoinTr achieves robust spatial generalization on diverse categories of manipulation tasks with only 20 action-labeled robot demonstrations. 3PoinTr outperforms the baselines, including behavior cloning methods, as well as prior methods for pretraining from human videos. We also provide evaluations of 3PoinTr's 3D point track predictions compared to an existing point track prediction baseline. We find that 3PoinTr produces more accurate and higher quality point tracks due to a lightweight yet expressive architecture built on a single transformer, in addition to a training formulation that preserves supervision of partially occluded points. Project page: https://adamhung60.github.io/3PoinTr/.

</details>


### [489] [An Open-Source Robotics Research Platform for Autonomous Laparoscopic Surgery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.08490)
*Ariel Rodriguez,Lorenzo Mazza,Martin Lelis,Rayan Younis,Sebastian Bodenstedt,Martin Wagner,Stefanie Speidel*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Open-source robot-agnostic RCM controller for surgical robotics with sub-millimeter precision, enabling teleoperation, data collection, and autonomous policy deployment across phantom, ex vivo, and in vivo procedures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing surgical robotics platforms (like da Vinci Research Kit) suffer from cable-driven mechanical limitations that degrade state-space consistency and hinder training of reliable autonomous policies. Need for reliable, high-precision platforms that adhere to safety and kinematic constraints of minimally invasive surgery.

Method: Developed open-source, robot-agnostic Remote Center of Motion (RCM) controller using closed-form analytical velocity solver that enforces trocar constraint deterministically without iterative optimization. Controller operates in Cartesian space, enabling any industrial manipulator to function as surgical robot. Implemented for UR5e and Franka Emika Panda with stereoscopic 3D perception. Full-stack ROS-based platform with decoupled server-client architecture supporting teleoperation, demonstration recording, and learned policy deployment.

Result: RCM deviations remained sub-millimeter across all conditions (phantom, ex vivo, in vivo porcine laparoscopic procedures). Trajectory smoothness metrics (SPARC, LDLJ) comparable to expert demonstrations from JIGSAWS benchmark recorded on da Vinci system. Successfully validated on bowel grasping and retraction task.

Conclusion: The platform provides the precision and robustness required for teleoperation, data collection, and autonomous policy deployment in realistic surgical scenarios, overcoming limitations of existing cable-driven systems while maintaining surgical-grade performance.

Abstract: Autonomous robot-assisted surgery demands reliable, high-precision platforms that strictly adhere to the safety and kinematic constraints of minimally invasive procedures. Existing research platforms, primarily based on the da Vinci Research Kit, suffer from cable-driven mechanical limitations that degrade state-space consistency and hinder the downstream training of reliable autonomous policies. We present an open-source, robot-agnostic Remote Center of Motion (RCM) controller based on a closed-form analytical velocity solver that enforces the trocar constraint deterministically without iterative optimization. The controller operates in Cartesian space, enabling any industrial manipulator to function as a surgical robot. We provide implementations for the UR5e and Franka Emika Panda manipulators, and integrate stereoscopic 3D perception. We integrate the robot control into a full-stack ROS-based surgical robotics platform supporting teleoperation, demonstration recording, and deployment of learned policies via a decoupled server-client architecture. We validate the system on a bowel grasping and retraction task across phantom, ex vivo, and in vivo porcine laparoscopic procedures. RCM deviations remain sub-millimeter across all conditions, and trajectory smoothness metrics (SPARC, LDLJ) are comparable to expert demonstrations from the JIGSAWS benchmark recorded on the da Vinci system. These results demonstrate that the platform provides the precision and robustness required for teleoperation, data collection and autonomous policy deployment in realistic surgical scenarios.

</details>


### [490] [Rethinking the semantic classification of indoor places by mobile robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.08512)
*Oscar Martinez Mozos,Alejandra C. Hernandez,Clara Gomez,Ramon Barber*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes allowing semantic classification confusion within rooms to help service robots better understand flexible space usage, with proof of concept in object search tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional room-level semantic segmentation fails when different areas within the same room serve different functions (e.g., kitchen table used as office). Service robots need to adapt to flexible human space usage patterns.

Method: New paradigm that intentionally relaxes semantic classifier labeling by allowing confusions inside rooms, rather than forcing strict room-level categorization.

Result: Proof of concept demonstrates that allowing semantic confusion within rooms can be beneficial for service robots, particularly in object search tasks.

Conclusion: Permitting semantic confusion within rooms rather than enforcing strict room-level categorization can improve service robot performance by better aligning with how humans actually use spaces flexibly.

Abstract: A significant challenge in service robots is the semantic understanding of their surrounding areas. Traditional approaches addressed this problem by segmenting the floor plan into regions corresponding to full rooms that are assigned labels consistent with human perception, e.g. office or kitchen. However, different areas inside the same room can be used in different ways: Could the table and the chair in my kitchen become my office? What is the category of that area now? office or kitchen? To adapt to these circumstances we propose a new paradigm where we intentionally relax the resulting labeling of semantic classifiers by allowing confusions inside rooms. Our hypothesis is that those confusions can be beneficial to a service robot. We present a proof of concept in the task of searching for objects.

</details>


### [491] [AtomVLA: Scalable Post-Training for Robotic Manipulation via Predictive Latent World Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.08519)
*Xiaoquan Sun,Zetian Xu,Chen Cao,Zonghe Liu,Yihan Sun,Jingrui Pang,Ruijian Zhang,Zhen Yang,Kang Pang,Dingxin He,Mingqi Yuan,Jiayu Chen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: AtomVLA: A subtask-aware VLA framework with scalable offline post-training that decomposes high-level tasks into atomic subtasks using LLMs and scores action chunks with a predictive world model to improve long-horizon robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VLA models rely on coarse high-level instructions during fine-tuning, creating an instruction grounding gap that leads to compounding errors in long-horizon tasks. There's an urgent need to bridge this gap with scalable post-training methods.

Method: Uses LLM to decompose high-level demonstrations into fine-grained atomic subtasks, then employs a pretrained predictive world model to score candidate action chunks against subtask goals in latent space. Enables efficient Group Relative Policy Optimization without expensive online robot rollouts.

Result: Achieves 97.0% average success rate on LIBERO benchmark and 48.0% on LIBERO-PRO benchmark. Maintains strong robustness under perturbations and demonstrates broad applicability across diverse real-world tasks on Galaxea R1 Lite platform.

Conclusion: AtomVLA effectively bridges the instruction grounding gap in VLA models, significantly improving long-horizon robustness through subtask decomposition and latent-space action scoring, while enabling scalable offline training without physical robot expenses.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models demonstrate remarkable potential for generalizable robotic manipulation. The execution of complex multi-step behaviors in VLA models can be improved by robust instruction grounding, a critical component for effective control. However, current paradigms predominantly rely on coarse, high-level task instructions during supervised fine-tuning. This instruction grounding gap leaves models without explicit intermediate guidance, leading to severe compounding errors in long-horizon tasks. Therefore, bridging this instruction gap and providing scalable post-training for VLA models is urgent. To tackle this problem, we propose \method, the first subtask-aware VLA framework integrated with a scalable offline post-training pipeline. Our framework leverages a large language model to decompose high-level demonstrations into fine-grained atomic subtasks. This approach utilizes a pretrained predictive world model to score candidate action chunks against subtask goals in the latent space, mitigating error accumulation while significantly improving long-horizon robustness. Furthermore, this approach enables highly efficient Group Relative Policy Optimization without the prohibitive expenses associated with online rollouts on physical robots. Extensive simulations validate that our AtomVLA maintains strong robustness under perturbations. When evaluated against fundamental baseline models, it achieves an average success rate of 97.0\% on the LIBERO benchmark and 48.0\% on the LIBERO-PRO benchmark. Finally, experiments conducted in the real world using the Galaxea R1 Lite platform confirm its broad applicability across diverse tasks, especially long-horizon tasks. All datasets, checkpoints, and code will be released to the public domain following the acceptance of this work for future research.

</details>


### [492] [CRED: Counterfactual Reasoning and Environment Design for Active Preference Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.08531)
*Yi-Shiuan Tung,Gyanig Kumar,Wei Jiang,Bradley Hayes,Alessandro Roncone*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: CRED is a novel active preference learning method that improves reward inference by jointly optimizing environment design and trajectory selection, using counterfactual reasoning to generate informative trajectory comparisons.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As robots operate in increasingly complex environments, manually specifying optimization objectives becomes infeasible. While active preference learning (APL) can learn human reward functions through trajectory rankings, existing methods suffer from limited query diversity and often fail to identify informative comparisons due to sampling from fixed trajectory sets or replay buffers.

Method: CRED proposes a trajectory generation method that jointly optimizes environment design and trajectory selection. It "imagines" new scenarios through environment design and uses counterfactual reasoning by sampling possible rewards from its current belief and asking "What if this were the true preference?" to generate trajectory pairs that expose differences between competing reward functions.

Result: Comprehensive experiments and a user study show that CRED significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods in reward accuracy and sample efficiency, and receives higher user ratings.

Conclusion: CRED represents an advancement in active preference learning by enabling more efficient and accurate reward inference through intelligent trajectory generation that leverages environment design and counterfactual reasoning to extract human preferences more effectively.

Abstract: As a robot's operational environment and tasks to perform within it grow in complexity, the explicit specification and balancing of optimization objectives to achieve a preferred behavior profile moves increasingly farther out of reach. These systems benefit strongly by being able to align their behavior to reflect human preferences and respond to corrections, but manually encoding this feedback is infeasible. Active preference learning (APL) learns human reward functions by presenting trajectories for ranking. However, existing methods sample from fixed trajectory sets or replay buffers that limit query diversity and often fail to identify informative comparisons. We propose CRED, a novel trajectory generation method for APL that improves reward inference by jointly optimizing environment design and trajectory selection to efficiently query and extract preferences from users. CRED "imagines" new scenarios through environment design and leverages counterfactual reasoning -- by sampling possible rewards from its current belief and asking "What if this were the true preference?" -- to generate trajectory pairs that expose differences between competing reward functions. Comprehensive experiments and a user study show that CRED significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods in reward accuracy and sample efficiency and receives higher user ratings.

</details>


### [493] [EquiBim: Learning Symmetry-Equivariant Policy for Bimanual Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.08541)
*Zhiyuan Zhang,Aditya Mohan,Seungho Han,Wan Shou,Dongyi Wang,Yu She*
